attributes.location_description: Rio Bonito do Iguaçu is a municipality located in the central-southern region of the state of Paraná, approximately 400 km from the state capital, Curitiba. The municipality has an estimated population of about 14,000 inhabitants and is characterized by economic activities based on agriculture and livestock farming.
attributes.description:
The tornado formed and struck the municipality of Rio Bonito do Iguaçu during the afternoon of November 7, 2025. According to the Paraná Environmental Technology and Monitoring System (SIMEPAR), the development of the tornado was enabled by the cold sector of an extratropical cyclone formed over southern Brazil, which favored the development of high-intensity thunderstorm clouds over the region. Some of these storm clouds, embedded in an environment of high thermodynamic instability, intensified further and evolved into supercells, characterized by rotation around their vertical axis. Strong vertical wind shear and the transport of warm, moist air were fundamental to the evolution of the storms. Two of these supercells were responsible for the occurrence of three tornadoes in municipalities in the state of Paraná. 

The first supercell generated Tornado 1, which passed through Rio Bonito do Iguaçu as an F4 tornado on the Fujita scale. This same supercell also generated Tornado 2, which passed through the Entre Rios District in Guarapuava, also as an F4 tornado. The supercell traveled approximately 270 km, with an average translational speed of about 80 km/h. A second supercell traveled approximately 230 km, with an average translational speed of about 85 km/h, and generated Tornado 3, which passed over Turvo as an F2 tornado. On the Fujita scale, F2 tornadoes are considered of considerable severity, with estimated wind speeds between 180 km/h and 253 km/h. F3 tornadoes are considered severe, with estimated wind speeds between 253 km/h and 332 km/h. F4 tornadoes are classified as devastating, with estimated wind speeds between 332 km/h and 418 km/h.

Typhoon “KALMAEGI (2525)” over the South China Sea made landfall in Binh Dinh, Vietnam at 08:00 p.m. on 6 Nov. The storm weakened into a tropical storm over Cambodia at 04:00 a.m. and further into a tropical depression over Laos at
07:00 a.m. before moving into Thailand at Sirindhorn District, Ubon Ratchathani province at 09:30 a.m. on 7 November, and eventually became an active low-pressure cell over Si Sa Ket province at 05:00 p.m. on the same day. The remnant of storm continued moving to the northeastern and northern regions during 8-9 November and covering Myanmar on the following day.

attributes.description:
La región costera de Ica, caracterizada por su marcada aridez, registró el 12 de febrero un evento de lluvia extrema e inusual, con acumulados sin precedentes en cuatro estaciones meteorológicas: Letrayoc (Pisco–Huancano) con 18.6 mm/día, San Camilo (Ica–Parcona) con 38.8 mm/día, Palpa (Palpa–Palpa) con 6.0 mm/día y Copara (Nazca–Alegre) con 17.8 mm/día. Estos valores superaron ampliamente los promedios mensuales, destacando anomalías del 989 % en Copara y del 1764 % en San Camilo respecto a su climatología mensual, considerando que la región normalmente recibe entre 0.5 mm y 6 mm de precipitación durante todo el mes. Previo a este evento, se venía desarrollando el periodo lluvioso en las zonas altas y medias colindantes a la región Ica, especialmente en el sur de Huancavelica y Ayacucho, con la formación de sistemas convectivos de gran desarrollo vertical. Asimismo, la temperatura superficial del mar frente y al sur de Ica se incrementó hasta valores entre 24 y 26 °C, favoreciendo la formación de nubes cálidas con alto contenido de humedad en la franja costera, las cuales interactuaron con la nubosidad proveniente de la sierra sur. Esta interacción propició la ocurrencia de la lluvia extrema del 12 de febrero, la cual superó el récord histórico previo de precipitación registrado en marzo de 1972.

- Ica rompió récords de lluvias intensas que destruyeron viviendas: diariocorreo.pe/edicion/ica/ica-rompio-records-de-lluvias-intensas-que-destruyeron-viviendas-noticia/ 
- Estado de las lluvias a nivel nacional 2024-2025: https://www.gob.pe/institucion/senamhi/informes-publicaciones/7130945-estado-de-las-lluvias-a-nivel-nacional-2024-2025

- Los eventos de lluvia intensa ocurridos en la región Ica generaron impactos significativos en la población, la vivienda, la infraestructura y el sector agrícola. En la provincia de Ica se registraron 42 personas damnificadas y 14 viviendas destruidas, mientras que en la provincia de Nasca se contabilizaron 4 000 personas afectadas, 1 500 viviendas afectadas y 6 km de camino rural afectados. En el distrito de El Ingenio se reportaron 4 000 personas afectadas y 1 500 viviendas afectadas, y en la provincia de Palpa se registraron 900 personas afectadas, 30 damnificadas, 450 viviendas afectadas y 4 km de camino rural afectados. Asimismo, se reportó un impacto en el sector agrícola con 200 hectáreas de cultivos afectados, evidenciando la magnitud de los daños ocasionados por este evento en la región Ica. https://portal.indeci.gob.pe/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/REPORTE-COMPLEMENTARIO-N.%C2%BA-1878-13FEB2025-LLUVIAS-INTENSAS-EN-EL-DEPARTAMENTO-DE-ICA-2-copia.pdf

The rainy season in North China began on July 5 and ended on September 2, lasting for 59 days. The cumulative rainfall reached 356.6 millimeters, 2.6 times higher than the annual average. This marks the earliest start, longest duration, and highest total rainfall since 1961. From July 23 to 29, severe heavy rainfall and flooding affected many parts of North China. In Xinglong, Chengde, Hebei, the total rainfall reached 650.1 millimeters, setting a local historical record. In Langfangyu, Miyun, Beijing, the maximum cumulative rainfall reached 573.5 millimeters, exceeding 90% of the local average annual rainfall. Continuous rainfall in Beijing lasted for 147 hours, surpassing the 83-hour duration of the "July 2023" event. Inner Mongolia experienced its strongest heavy rainfall process in terms of comprehensive intensity on record.

Typhoon Ragasa reached its peak intensity at above 62 m/s and maintained super typhoon status for over 81 hours, significantly surpassing the duration of last year's super typhoon "Yagi" (64 hours). It was the strongest typhoon in the Northwest Pacific this year, with its cloud system spanning over 1,000 kilometers in diameter. Typhoon Ragasa made landfall as a typhoon in Yangjiang, Guangdong on September 24, and subsequently struck Guangxi twice on the 25th—first in Beihai and then in Fangchenggang. It brought heavy rains and strong winds to South China, East China, and Taiwan Province. Seawater backflow was notably severe in Zhuhai, Guangdong, while Taishan City in Jiangmen recorded a maximum gust of 67 m/s, setting a new local historical record.

From June 30 to September 9, China experienced its strongest heatwave of the year, lasting 71 days and ranking as the third-longest duration since 1961. Regions including Jianghuai, Jiangnan, Chongqing, and southern Xinjiang recorded 30 to 50 days of high temperatures. Daily maximum temperatures reached or exceeded historical extremes at 56 national weather stations, such as Dongkan in Turpan, Xinjiang (48.7°C) and Wuxi, Chongqing (44.2°C).

Under the influence of the rather active northeast monsoon prevailed over the Gulf of Thailand and the southern region, a low-pressure area covered the Gulf of Thailand and the lower southern region during 19–21 November and moved to cover the lower Andaman Sea and Malaysia during 22–25 November. In addition, the monsoon trough lay across the lower southern region during 24–25 November. These caused torrential rain with severe flooding in southern Thailand. Notably, Hat Yai, Kho Hong, and Sadao District in Songkhla Province, together with Pattani, Yala, and Narathiwat provinces, recorded cumulative rainfall totals exceeding 1,000 mm during 17–25 November 2025. The highest 1-day rainfall during this period was 624.5 mm, recorded at Mueang District, Narathiwat Province, on 22 November 2025.
Affected households has risen to 94, with more than 467 individuals directly impacted by the ongoing rainfall.

A very severe and long-lasting early summer heatwave, accompanied by severe flash drought as June was the driest recorded in many regions. Mean air temperature over the 15-day period exceeded the corresponding long-term mean by 4 or 5 °C at most places. At some stations an anomaly of 10 °C was measured for the hottest day of the period. 

Many stations measured record high temperature for June on June 26, for example in Dobliče near Črnomelj (38.4 °C - new national high for Slovenia in June), 37.9 °C in Metlika, 36.9 °C in Celje, and 36.7 °C in Novo mesto. On the same day also daily mean temperature broke the June record at places: e.g. 31.5 °C in Dobliče near Črnomelj, 29.7 °C in Celje, and 29.0 in Ljubljana. 

On July 2-4 there was another peak of the heatwave, comparable to the first or sligthly lower.

During the heatwave some severe thunderstorms with hail and strong downpours occured.

attributes.description: Between 13:30 and 14:00 CEST northern half of Goričko region (part of northeastern Slovenia) crossed a supercell thunderstorm, accompanied by large hail, reaching or exceeding 6 cm in diameter in places.

Extremely dry period from May 29 to June 25 with rainfall in most of Slovenia below 40 mm and only 1 mm or so in a regions south of Ljubljana and in part of Bela krajina region. No drier periods in many places have been recorded so far in this time of year. The longterm mean rainfall for the period is 80-120 mm in lowlands and around 200 mm in the Julian Alps. In parts of Slovenia there were some showers or downpours in late June and early July, whereas in other places the first abundant rainfall was on July 7. Later drought situation quickly improved due to frequent and abundant rainfall.

attributes.description: Training thunderstorms from early morning till late afternoon. Individual cells moved towards northeast, where the belt shifted only for 10 or 20 km. In some places more than 100 or even 200 mm of rain fell in several hours. The official automatic weather station in Postojna received 185 mm in less than 12 hours, highly surpassing the estimated 100-yr return level for that duration. The daily amount of rainfall at manual station in Postojna was  204 mm, highly surpassing the previous record of 139 mm, measured on September 21, 1933. To the southwest, stations of Kozina and Dekani recorded daily amount of 160 and 175 mm, respectively.

June 2025 was the second warmest since beginning of measurements in 1864, with a deviation of 3.8 °C from the reference period 1991-2020, behind the record June of 2003. In mountainous areas, the June average temperature climbed 4.5 °C above the reference values. On the Jungfraujoch, June 2025 was even the warmest since records began over 90 years ago, with a monthly average temperature of 2.6 °C, surpassing the previous record set during the hot summer of 2003 (2.2 °C).
June owed its warmth to three cycles lasting several days, during which the daily maximum temperatures in the lowlands rose well above 30 °C. Locally, between 5 and 14 hot days were recorded, with occasional tropical nights. In June, the zero degree line rose to 5124.6 m in a single day for the first time since measurements began in 1954. Temperatures remained high also during the first week of July.

attributes.description: Over the course of about a week, a southerly flow brought extremely mild conditions north of the Alps. Around Lake Geneva, in the foehn valleys and in the northernmost parts of Switzerland, maximum temperatures rose to values above 15 °C. In Geneva, a new January record for daily maximum temperature was observed on January 25th with 18,1 °C. This measurement series was started in 1864.

attributes.location_description: North of the Alps, along the norther Alpine slopes, mainly in the western and central parts of Switzerland
attributes.description:
An active cold front led to heavy precipitation in parts of Switzerland and brought substantial amounts of fresh snow in the western parts of the Swiss Alps. On January 27, many stations with long measurement series recorded the highest amount of daily precipitation for the month of January since the beginning of measurements. At these stations, between 50 and 80 mm of precipitation were observed with 24 hours. 
During the southerly flow conditions from 24 to 27 January and the subsequent cold front, plenty of fresh snow fell in the mountains on the southern side of the Alps. On San Bernardino at 1640 m, the snow cover grew from around 50 cm to over 110 cm and values high above the climatological average of the period 1991-2020.
The heavy rainfall resulted in one of the five wettest Januaries since records began at almost 27 measuring stations with longer series of measurements. In the Thun series of measurements, available since 1875, it was the wettest January, together with January 1986, with 100 mm of rainfall. It was also the wettest January in the Guglera (available since 1961) and Plaffeien (available since 1972) measurement series. Guglera received a January total of 159 mm, while Plaffeien received 140 mm.

Extreme lack of precipitation in Eastern Switzerland: At the Altstätten measuring station in the St. Gallen Rhine Valley, the lack of precipitation from February to April reached extreme levels. The three-month total of 72.3 mm was the lowest ever recorded in the comparable period of the past 90 years. The record minimum (February to April) of 68.6 mm dates back to 1934. These are the two lowest values since measurements began in 1864.
In addition to eastern Switzerland, which was particularly affected, precipitation totals from February to April also remained significantly below the 1991-2020 norm in large parts of the rest of Switzerland. The southern side of the Alps mostly received amounts in line with or slightly above the norm. In the areas of Valais affected by the April storms, however, precipitation totals from February to April rose well above the 1991–2020 norm.
The lack of precipitation in recent months led to extremely low snow cover in the mountains in the first third of May. On the Weissfluhjoch (2691 m) in the Eastern Alps of Switzerland, the average snow depth from 1 to 10 May was only 89 cm. This is the lowest value for this period since measurements began in 1959. In second place is the first third of May 2007 with 95 cm. In most other years, the average snow depth on the Weissfluhjoch in the first third of May reached more than 1.5 m. Last year, 2024, one of the highest snow depths at this time of year was recorded here at just under 2.5 m.

attributes.location_description: Whole of Switzerland, but largest precipitation deficit in the Alps
attributes.description: In parts of the Grisons and Valais cantons, only 10% of the February norm 1991-2020 or less fell locally. In Susch in the Lower Engadine, the monthly total of 2 mm ranked second in the list of the driest February months since measurements began in 1901. Thusis in central Grisons recorded 3 mm, ranking fourth since measurements began in 1892. Binn in the Upper Valais reported a monthly total of 5.4 mm, ranking fourth since measurements began in 1900.

The persistent high pressure conditions in the first third of the month were replaced by a five-day period of low pressure starting on 13 April. This brought heavy rainfall to the southern side of the Alps, Valais and the Bernese Oberland from 16 to 17 April with a strong south-easterly flow. In Valais, this also fell as fresh snow down to low altitudes.
In western Ticino and the Simplon region, the highest measured 1-day precipitation totals reached 140 to just under 170 mm. In central Valais, the highest measured values were between 130 and 160 mm. In the Bernese Oberland, individual measuring stations recorded 100 to just over 120 mm.
Ten measuring stations in Valais and three in the Bernese Oberland recorded their highest 1-day totals since measurements began. These include the measuring stations in Leukerbad (119 mm), Kandersteg (124 mm) and Lauterbrunnen (105 mm), which have been recording measurements for over 120 years. At five measuring stations in Valais and in Kandersteg in the Bernese Oberland, such a 1-day precipitation event is expected to occur less than once every 100 years. At several other measuring stations, it was an event that is only expected to occur every 50 to 100 years. 
During the heavy precipitation episode, a cold front from the west brought a cooling effect. In addition, precipitation cooling set in in the Rhone Valley and parts of the Bernese Oberland. This occurred at the time when the precipitation reached its maximum intensity during the night of 16 to 17 April. When precipitation cools, heat is extracted from the environment as the snowflakes melt. This cools the air mass and lowers the snow line. Precipitation cooling is particularly pronounced in the valleys. In a large part of the Rhone Valley and also in the Bernese Oberland, a snow cover of remarkable thickness in some places formed by the morning of 17 April, even at low altitudes: Sion reported 9 cm, Siders 25 cm and Visp 30 cm. At higher altitudes, the new snow depths reached 60 to 100 cm. The heavy snowfall led to road and rail closures in some areas of Valais, as well as prolonged power outages. 
Locally, new April records were set for 1-day new snow totals. In Montana, 58 cm fell. The previous April record from 1962 was 50 cm. In Adelboden, 52 cm of new snow fell between 16 and 17 April. The previous April record from 1976 was 50 cm. Snow measurement data for Montana is available from 1931 onwards, and for Adelboden from 1966 onwards.

attributes.location_description: northern and southern side of the Alps was affected
attributes.description:
A low-pressure system centred over the British Isles brought a cold front across Switzerland during the night of 21 July and the following day. Ahead of this cold front, several thunderstorms had already passed over the northern side of the Alps on Sunday evening. During the course of 22 July, the low-pressure system over the British Isles brought cold air at high altitude and a partial trough over Switzerland. This meant that the air mass remained unstable, and the uplift caused by the partial trough triggered further showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon. Between Sunday evening and Monday lunchtime, 40 to 60 mm of precipitation was recorded on the central and eastern northern slopes of the Alps and in northern and central Graubünden, with localised amounts of up to 80 mm in the Alpstein. Due to the convective nature of the precipitation, large regional differences in precipitation amounts were observed, particularly south of the Alps. Between 100 and 130 mm of precipitation fell from Locarno to the central Maggia Valley and the Magadino Plain to the Riviera, with localised amounts of up to 150 mm, and between 40 and 60 mm in the upper Maggia Valley, Misox, Blenio Valley and Bregaglia. On 21 July 2025, an hourly precipitation total of 51.5 mm was measured at the Magadino/Cadenazzo station, which is the fifth highest hourly total since measurements began in 1981.

https://www.meteoschweiz.admin.ch/ueber-uns/meteoschweiz-blog/de/2025/07/bilanz-des-intensiven-dauerregens.html

Between 23 and 25 July, a low-pressure system slowly moving from the English Channel to the Mediterranean once again brought very unstable air towards the Alps. On 24 July, a violent thunderstorm (supercell) passed over Sottoceneri, causing extensive damage in Lugano due to strong winds. The Stabio station in Mendrisiotto recorded 23.6 mm of rain within 10 minutes, the second highest value for the month of July since measurements began in 1981 and the fourth highest value in the entire measurement period.

From 26 to 29 July, an extensive low-pressure zone over Central Europe, combined with moist and unstable air, led to heavy rainfall on the northern side of the Alps. A total of 80 to 120 mm of precipitation fell on the northern slopes of the Alps within three days, with some areas receiving around 150 mm. On 26 July in particular, there was very intense and, in some cases, exceptional rainfall over a short period of time in certain regions. At the Bözberg measuring station, 76 mm of precipitation was recorded within just one hour. This is one of the highest 1-hour precipitation amounts ever recorded on the northern side of the Alps.

https://www.meteoschweiz.admin.ch/ueber-uns/meteoschweiz-blog/de/2025/07/kraeftige-schauer-und-gewitter-ergiebige-niederschlaege-und-kuehle-julitage.html

From 7 to 18 August, average temperatures in August 2025 were very high. They were also among the two to five warmest 10-day periods in August since measurements began, according to MeteoSwiss' long measurement series. In Lugano, this August saw the second warmest 10-day period since measurements began in 1864, with an average temperature of 26.2 °C. The same temperature was already reached in August 2023. The record value in Lugano from 2003 was 1.1 °C higher, while last year's fourth place was only slightly below this year's value. Average temperatures above 25 °C over ten days were rarely reached in Lugano before 2000, but are now the rule rather than the exception.
In Bern/Zollikofen and Geneva/Cointrin, the warmest 10-day period this August ranked third since measurements began. Here, 2023 and 2003 were significantly warmer. Even at higher altitudes, the warmest 10-day period this August was among the five warmest on record, for example on the Säntis and in Samedan. All measuring stations show a significant increase in warm 10-day periods in August over the last 20 years.
The heatwave did not set any new records for daily maximum temperatures. Only in Adelboden was the previous record of 29.5 °C set on 24 August 2023 equalled on 9 August. At low altitudes, the record values from 2003 and 2023, with temperatures above 37 °C, were not reached anywhere. The highest values in August ranged between 35 and 36.4 °C.
Isolated records were also broken again for daily minimum temperatures in mountainous areas: at the Pilatus measuring station, the record value of 17.1 °C was broken again after August 2012, and at the Grimsel Hospice, the 14.5 °C recorded in August 2023 was equalled. Only the Gütsch Andermatt station reported a new record minimum temperature of 15.2 °C. The old record of 14.9 °C was also set in 2023.

The heatwave was exceptional, affecting 63% of stations on 28/06, with maximum temperatures exceeding 40°C in several regions on June 29: 47.4°C in Taroudant and in Sidi Slimane, 46.1°C in Marrakech, 46.9°C in Smara, and 42°C in Khouribga. and again on July 04, the heat persisted with 45.9°C in Smara and 44°C in Guelmim. Additionally, four cities recorded new June records: Casablanca reached 40.5°C on June 29 (previous record 38.6°C on 25/06/2011), Larache recorded 43.8°C on June 28 (previous record 42.9°C on 17/06/2017), El Jadida reached 39.3°C on June 29 (previous record 39.1°C on 25/06/2012), and Benguerir recorded 46.4°C on June 28 (previous record 45.3°C on 27/06/2023).

The heatwave was unusual, affecting 48% of stations, with temperatures soaring on July 16 to 45.8°C in Smara, 45°C in Guelmim, 47.2°C in Taroudant, 41.3°C in Safi, and 45°C in Marrakech, but without breaking any records.

The heatwave was exceptional due to its 13-day duration and extreme heat, affecting 63% of stations on August 10 and 11, with temperatures reaching 46.9°C in Taroudant and 47.2°C in Smara on August 10, then 45.7°C in Marrakech and 48°C in Taroudant on August 11. Temperatures rose to 47.9°C in Sidi Slimane and 45.2°C in Marrakech on August 12. On August 16, Temperatures reached 47.7°C in Taroudant, 42.7°C in Fes, and 43.3°C in Safi. On August 17, temperatures reached 42.8°C in Tangier, 47.4°C in Sidi Slimane, 45.6°C in Marrakech, 46.3°C in Taroudant, and 46.4°C in Smara. Laayoune set a new August record with a high of 49.2°C on August 9, exceeding the previous record of 47°C on August 5, 2016.

The heatwave was unusual, affecting 74% of stations on 19 September, with temperatures reaching 42°C in Sidi Slimane, 34.5°C in Casablanca, 40.9°C in Agadir, and 44.3°C in Smara. On 14 September, Taroudant also recorded 43.2°C, but without breaking any records.

20-40 cm of snow fell in eastern Lapland and in the region of Koillismaa within a couple of days. In Värriötunturi, Salla, the snow depth increased even by 45 cm from the morning of 3 May to the morning of 4 May, marking a new station-specific record for the daily snowfall amount. Moreover, it set the new national monthly record for daily snowfall level in May in Finland.

There was an intense and prolonged heatwave in Finland in the summer of 2025 lasting from 11 July to 5 August. In Meltosjärvi, Ylitornio in southwestern Lapland, the maximum temperature rose above 25 °C on 26 consecutive days which is, although not a national record, but a regional record for the region of Lapland. Moreover, from 12 July to 2 August, i.e., on 22 consecutive days, at least one weather station in Finland reported a maximum temperature above 30 °C. This is the longest continuous streak of such a warm days in Finland on record.

It was the warmest September on record in the region of Lapland in northern Finland. In Sodankylä, for instance, the monthly mean temperature, 11.0 °C, surpassed the previous record from September 2024 by a margin of 0.7 °C. The continuous temperature measurements in Sodankylä started in 1908. According to our attribution study, the mean temperature of September was 1.9 °C higher due to global warming since 1900. Moreover, we estimated that in the current climate the recurrence interval of such a warm September is approximately once in 90 years, while without the global warming the recurrence interval would be several thousands of years.

Vigorous windstorm Hannes affected western and northern Finland on 27 December. Based on the caused impacts, it was the third most significant large-scale windstorm in Finland during the 21st century. Gust wind speeds peaking between 25-30 m/s were observed widely in the inland areas in western and northern Finland.

Very heavy rain in coastal areas of central Sweden. 140.0 mm on 6 September at climate station Myckelgensjö, second highest daily rain amount in Sweden for the month of September. Only an event in September 1913 with slightly higher reported daily amounts.

Wind storm named Johannes by Swedish Meteorological and Hydrological Institute. Deep low centered over Finland with strong and gusty northwesterly winds.
Eight stations in central Sweden reported new records för highest wind gusts. Estimated return period for wind gusts more than 100 years for 4 stations in Central Sweden.

attributes.location_description: Gaborone is the capital and largest city of Botswana, situated in the southeastern part of the country near the border with South Africa. It serves as the administrative, economic and political center of Botswana and is characterized by urban settlements, government offices and key infrastructure. The city lies within a semi-arid region with hot summers and mild winters and is surrounded by low-lying hills and savannah landscapes.
attributes.description:
In mid-February 2025 (16–20 February), southern Botswana experienced an extreme rainfall event that led to severe flooding, particularly in Gaborone. This makes the February 2025 rainfall one of the most significant multi-day rainfall events recorded in southern Botswana since at least the 1950s. The heavy rainfall caused widespread disruption, resulting in at least nine fatalities in Botswana, including six children, and the displacement of over 5,000 people. Critical infrastructure was severely affected, with roads and drainage systems overwhelmed,  schools temporarily closed nationwide and several communities cut off from emergency services. The event coincided with similar flooding impacts in eastern South Africa, highlighting its regional scale.

The flooding was driven by an exceptionally intense 5-day rainfall period. Even under today’s climate conditions, which are already about 1.3 °C warmer than pre-industrial levels, this type of rainfall event is rare. Analysis of station data for Gaborone indicates a return period of approximately 40–50 years, meaning such an event has only a 2–3% chance of occurring in any given year. 

Historical analysis shows a strong upward trend in the intensity of extreme 5-day rainfall events since 1950. Compared to a climate that was 1.3 °C cooler, rainfall of this magnitude is estimated to be about 60% more intense today. While climate models vary in their estimates, the combined observational evidence, physical understanding of warmer air holding more moisture, and future projections strongly indicate that human-induced climate change amplified the rainfall that led to the flooding.

Although flooding has occurred historically in Gaborone, the impacts of the February 2025 event were significantly worsened by rapid urbanization and drainage infrastructure that has not kept pace with population growth. Low-lying urban areas were particularly vulnerable. This event stands out as one of the most damaging urban flood events in Botswana in recent decades and underscores the increasing risk posed by extreme rainfall under a warming climate.

attributes.description:
El evento de temporal de lluvia, con una duración de tres días, fue generado por un sistema de baja presión ubicado frente a la costa de El Salvador, el cual se fortaleció posteriormente a la Tormenta Tropical Erick, al sur de Guatemala, desplazándose con trayectoria hacia las costas de México.

Este sistema favoreció el acercamiento y la activación de la Zona de Convergencia Intertropical (ZCIT), lo que intensificó las condiciones lluviosas y dio lugar al evento de precipitaciones descrito.

Se registraron lluvias a escala nacional, siendo la zona oriental del país la más afectada. El mayor acumulado de lluvia en 24 horas, con un valor de 198.2 mm, se registró en la estación Conchagua, ubicada al sur del departamento de La Unión, en las cercanías del Golfo de Fonseca. Asimismo, el acumulado máximo total del evento alcanzó 259.6 mm en la misma estación, correspondiente al periodo de tres días.

Durante este periodo, se registraron tormentas fuertes y lluvias diarias que favorecieron un periodo húmedo y lluvioso, que favoreciendo que las lluvias durante el mes de octubre fueran arriba del promedio y alcanzando escenario Arriba de lo Normal

A state of emergency was declared in Selwyn District, Christchurch, and Banks Peninsula, with significant flooding causing dozens of road closures. Akaroa recorded 223 mm of rain on 30 April: its wettest 1-day rainfall since records began in 1977, and equivalent to 23% of the town’s normal annual rainfall total.

Heavy rain caused widespread flooding in Tasman, Nelson, and Marlborough, and a state of emergency was declared for these regions. Several rural communities were evacuated, and approximately 60 northern South Island roads were closed.

Persistent heavy rainfall caused significant flooding in the Tasman region. Dozens of homes suffered damage and at least four were red-stickered (i.e. access was prohibited), with 21 yellow-stickered (i.e. access was restricted). Approximately 50 local roads were closed, and about 13,000 homes across Motueka and Golden Bay lost power. The Nelson-Tasman region was placed under a state of emergency, which remained in place until 17 July.

A state of emergency was declared, which lasted for two weeks. The wind knocked out power to more than 25,000 properties in Southland and Clutha. Invercargill City Council estimated more than 1,000 trees were potentially damaged. Farther north, roofs were damaged and over 1,600 properties were without power due to severe winds in Hanmer Springs (inland Canterbury).

A wildfire burned through approximately 2,800 hectares in Tongariro National Park. Thirty-six residents of Whakapapa Village were evacuated, as were hikers, lodges, and the Hillary Outdoors Centre. All tracks and huts within the Tongariro National Park were closed, while local highways were also closed. From 8-9 December, another fire in the same area additionally burned through approximately 322 hectares.

Severe thunderstorms, locally with large hail: On 4 September 2025, Switzerland experienced heavy thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening. Ahead of a sharp upper-level trough, moist and unstable air flowed from the southwest toward the northern side of the Alps. The first strong cell produced peak wind gusts of 80 km/h along the southern foothills of the Jura. Subsequently, further thunderstorms developed across the Swiss Plateau and along the Pre-Alps, increasingly forming larger clusters and, in addition to a large number of lightning strikes, causing locally heavy rainfall. At the Winterthur/Seen measuring station, a 10-minute rainfall total of 21.4 mm was recorded, while 16.4 mm was measured in Ebnat-Kappel. Over the course of the day, widespread precipitation amounts of 10–30 mm were observed, with local totals of 40–50 mm. A particularly tall thunderstorm cell reaching a height of 14 km and producing hail struck the Appenzell region, locally bringing hailstones measuring 2–4 cm in diameter.

attributes.location_description: South side of the Swiss Alps
attributes.description: Heavy precipitation on the southern side of the Alps: A cold front crossed the northern side of the Alps from 21 to 22 September 2025. Already ahead of the cold front, heavy precipitation occurred in the southern Valais Alps, on the southern side of the Alps, as well as along the central and northeastern Alpine slopes. In total, within 24 hours, widespread rainfall amounts of 70 to 110 mm were recorded in Ticino and the adjacent southern valleys in the canton of Grisons, with 120 to 190 mm falling in parts of central and southern Ticino. At the Torricella/Crana measuring site, around 188 mm were measured, corresponding there to a 5–10-year return period event. In the adjacent areas along the main Alpine ridge and in the inner-alpine regions, only 30 to 70 mm were recorded. Toward northern and eastern Grisons, precipitation totals were as low as 5 to 20 mm. On 23 and 24 September, convective precipitation continued locally across Switzerland, before conditions became somewhat drier again toward the end of the month.

From 20 October onward, a westerly flow dominated the upper atmosphere. After several wet days that locally brought between 15 and 35 mm of precipitation to western and northern Switzerland and to Valais, the first autumn storm of the current year arrived on 23 October 2025 with an active cold front. The front also produced storm winds over the lowlands: maximum gusts of 128 km/h were recorded in Bouveret, 114 km/h in Lucerne, 111 km/h in Nyon, 109 km/h in Thun, 106 km/h in Grenchen and Schaffhausen, and 102 km/h in Basel/Binningen and Zurich/Fluntern.

The highest wind gusts, ranging from 130 to 150 km/h, were measured in the Alps and the Jura. On Säntis, the anemometer recorded 149 km/h, and on Pilatus 148 km/h. On the northern side of the Alps, gusts of this magnitude during autumn storms are exceeded at many measuring sites approximately every 1–3 years or every 2–5 years. At some locations in the Lake Geneva region and in central Switzerland, return periods of 3–8 or even 10–20 years were recorded.

In Bière, at 684 m above sea level, a new maximum value of 125 km/h was measured, the highest since 1993, corresponding there to a 10–20-year return period according to extreme-value statistics. The value measured in Bouveret is exceeded roughly every 8–15 years. The stations at Lucerne, Pilatus, Nyon, and Pully recorded 3–8-year events. The front also brought a small amount of fresh snow to the mountains, with isolated totals of 10–20 cm (for example on Säntis, in Arosa, at Grimsel Hospiz, or at Weissfluhjoch).

Prolonged heavy rainfall affected the Mtwara Region from 2 to 7 February 2025 and caused severe flooding across the Region. At Mtwara Meteorological Station, a cumulative rainfall total of 396.1 mm was recorded over the five-day period. Significant heavy rainfall events occurred on 3 February (141.0 mm) and 6 February (95.4 mm). The 141.0 mm recorded on 3 February ranks as the third-highest daily rainfall for February since the commencement of observations in April 1950.

At the nearby Naliendele Agrometeorological Station, a total rainfall of 423.1 mm was recorded over the same period. Notably, significant daily rainfall amounts of 120.6 mm and 137.3 mm occurred on 3 February and 6 February, respectively. The 137.3 mm recorded on 6 February ranks as the seventh-highest daily rainfall on record and the second-highest daily rainfall for February since observations began in July 1980.
Severe thunderstorm activity was observed over Ushirombo Ward, resulting in injuries and loss of life.

Heavy rainfall was recorded over Songea District within a 24-hour period on 15 January 2025. A total of 110 mm of rainfall was measured at Songea Meteorological Station. This amount ranks as the third-highest daily rainfall ever recorded at the station and the highest daily rainfall for the month of January since observations began.

On 23rd March 2025, Musoma Municipal and adjacent areas in Mara Region experienced prolonged thunderstorms associated with heavy rainfall and strong winds. A total of 34.7 mm of rainfall was recorded during the event at the Musoma meteorological station. The thunderstorms caused significant disruption to livelihood activities, including damage to small businesses and livestock, and resulted in food losses.

On 6 May 2025, flooding occurred in Mbokomu Ward, Moshi District, following several days of continuous rainfall over the area and its surroundings. Rainfall observations from nearby meteorological stations in the Kilimanjaro Region indicate that persistent, though generally light to moderate, rainfall was recorded for five consecutive days preceding the event. Notably, the Lyamungo station recorded a cumulative rainfall total of 84.6 mm, ranking as the ninth-highest May rainfall amount within the recent record period beginning in 2000.

From 21 to 24 March 2025, Ulanga District in the Morogoro Region—particularly Iragua Ward—experienced continuous heavy rainfall accompanied by strong winds. These conditions resulted in severe flooding that destroyed 433 houses and displaced 2,165 people. Rainfall observations from the nearby Mahenge station indicate that persistent rainfall had already begun on 17 March 2025, contributing to soil saturation and worsening the flood impacts.

Heavy rainfall over the central, western, and southwestern parts of the country from 26 to 28 March caused flooding in Kilosa District, resulting in to the overflow of the Mkondoa River, which flooded settlements in Tindiga Village. The flooding was driven by persistent heavy rainfall in upstream catchment areas, particularly in Dodoma, Morogoro, and Manyara Regions, which drain into the Mkondoa catchment. Rainfall observations show that Dodoma experienced continuous heavy rainfall for at least three days prior to the event.

attributes.location_description: The affected areas included northeastern parts of the country including, Tanga, North Morogoro, and Kilimanjaro.
attributes.description: The highest daily maximum temperatures were predominantly observed during the third dekad of February, with pronounced extremes over the northeastern parts of the country. Ilonga Meteorological Station recorded the highest daily maximum temperature of 38.6 °C on 18 February 2025, representing the second occurrence of this value since observations began in January 1961; the previous occurrence was on 19 March 1981. Mlingano Station also experienced exceptionally high temperatures, recording 38.0 °C on 24 February 2025 and 37.8 °C on 25 February 2025, which are the highest and second-highest February temperatures since the station became operational in January 1938. Furthermore, Kilimanjaro International Airport (KIA) and Moshi stations recorded daily maximum temperatures of 37.9 °C on 25 February and 37.4 °C on 27 February 2025, respectively. These temperatures rank among the eight highest daily maximum temperature events recorded at these stations during the month of February since observations began in January 1972 (KIA) and April 1932 (Moshi).

Heavy rainfall was recorded at Pemba Meteorological Station on 28 October 2025. This event ranks as the 11th highest rainfall ever observed at the station and represents the highest rainfall recorded in the month of October since the station was established in February 1974.

attributes.location_description: Settlements and resorts along the southern part of the Black Sea Coast
attributes.description:
As a result of intense rainfall, several flash and riverine floods occurred in some of the southern Black Sea rivers basins and coastal areas. Nessebar and Tsarevo municipalities declared an emergency status as many houses, hotels, commercial properties and infrastructure were flooded; a partial emergency state was also declared in the city of Burgas due to local floodings. Elenite resort was most affected, suffering a severe flash flood.
The precipitation amounts in Burgas and the regions south of it are mainly for the interval from about 19:00 on 02.10.2025 to 07:00 on 03.10.2025 (about 11 hours). They are the largest in the area of ​​the village of Kosti – 250.7 mm (l/m²), which is 208% of the monthly climatic norm. The following precipitation amounts were measured in the other meteorological stations of the NIMH: Burgas – 110.3 mm (over 169% of the norm) and Veselie village – 145.6 mm (154% of the norm).
The course of precipitation in the area of ​​the town of Nessebar is slightly different - there, according to this process, it starts raining around 23:00 on 02.10.2025 and stops shortly after 14:00 on 03.10.2025 (a little over 15 hours). The measured amount according to the process (measured within 2 days) is a total of 95.9 mm (l/m²) or 195% of the monthly norm. In reality, as a ratio to the monthly norm, this amount is comparable to the precipitation in the village of Kosti, although as a total precipitation it is more than 2 times smaller.
The precipitation measured during this process on 03.10.2025 in the village of Kosti is the absolute maximum for the maximum annual 24-hour precipitation for the entire measurement period since 1948 - 250.7 mm.

March 2025 was Singapore's wettest March on record, with islandwide average rainfall of 482.9mm, 130% above the month's long-term average, while the climate station's monthly total rainfall of 550.1mm broke the previous climate station March record of 528.3mm from 1913.

The first snowfall down to low altitudes arrived with a series of cold fronts between 17 and 25 November. During this phase, temperatures regularly dropped below freezing even at low elevations. From 18 to 21 November, daily mean temperatures were widely 3 to 5 °C below the long-term average of the 1991–2020 reference period, and in mountainous regions up to 11 °C below. On the coldest days, from 21 to 23 November, daily mean temperatures fell almost nationwide to 5–10 °C below the reference period, again with lower values in the Alps. Only in the valleys of Ticino and along the Ticino lakes did temperatures remain somewhat closer to the 1991–2020 average.

Nationwide, the coldest day during this period was 22 November. At many locations, daily mean temperatures were recorded that are only undercut every 3–8 or 5–10 years. For the current climate, the daily mean on 22 November was exceptionally low at several measuring stations, with return periods of 8–15, 10–25 years, or even longer. La Brévine in the Jura, officially the coldest location in the MeteoSwiss measurement network with a record temperature of −41.8 °C (from 12 January 1987), recorded a daily mean of −17.0 °C. At this site, such a value now represents an event occurring only once every 25 years in November. The same return period applied to the daily mean of −4.2 °C measured in Schaffhausen.

The cold during the night from 22 to 23 November set a new record for the daily minimum temperature at a station with measurements dating back to 1971: in Ebnat-Kappel, −15.7 °C was recorded, the coldest November temperature ever observed at this site.

Persistent high-pressure conditions led to a lack of precipitation in December 2025. Only from 5 to 8 December on the northern side of the Alps, and from 16 to 18 December on the southern side of the Alps, were there two precipitation events with moderate amounts. Otherwise, hardly any significant precipitation fell during the first two thirds of December.

In most regions of Switzerland, precipitation totals remained below 35% of the December average of the 1991–2020 reference period by the end of the month, in some places even in the single-digit percentage range (e.g. Pilatus with 7%, Grimsel Hospiz with 4%).

The lack of precipitation was accompanied by a widespread and pronounced snow deficit. By the end of the month, this deficit was particularly marked at mid elevations and in lowland areas.

The total monthly precipitation in December 2025 reached second place among the driest December months at a measuring station with more than 130 years of data: Appenzell recorded only 9.9 mm (record: 5.0 mm in 2016).

attributes.location_description: Le nord, le centre et le sud est
attributes.description: Between the 11th and 18th of the month, a low-pressure system settled over the entire country, causing rainfall in the north, central, and southeastern regions, along with a notable drop in temperatures, especially in the western highlands and southwest. During this weather disturbance, two new rainfall records were broken: 80 mm on the 17th in Tabarka and 36.7 mm on the 16th in Remada. Other significant amounts were also recorded in Tabarka, with 51.6 mm on the 12th and 56.4 mm on the 13th.

A widespread heavy rainfall event affected several regions of Tunisia, with a peak around 7 February. Significant rainfall amounts were recorded across the central, coastal, and southern areas, with the highest totals observed at Matmata Airport (57.6 mm), Tozeur (52.4 mm), Monastir (48.0 mm), and Kairouan (43.2 mm).

The autumn 2025 season was marked by several episodes of heavy and locally intense rainfall in Tunisia. Analysis of daily precipitation totals shows that the most significant events were concentrated in the north and northwest, with daily amounts exceeding 30 mm/24 h. Central and southern regions experienced more irregular rainfall, though sometimes significant.
High daily precipitation totals were notably recorded at:
• Tabarka: 27 September (45 mm), 2 November (33.4 mm), 21 November (72 mm), 22 November (42.2 mm), 26 November (46.2 mm), 27 November (38 mm), 28 November (34 mm)
• Béja: 18 October (31.7 mm)
• Bizerte: 21 November (39 mm)
• Sidi Bouzid: 19 October (33.7 mm)
• Djerba: 20 November (27.4 mm, a moderate but notable event)

attributes.location_description: Wildfires in multiple Canadian provinces: British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Newfoundland. Wildfire smoke spread over vast areas and degraded air quality intermittently across much of Canada and northern United States.
attributes.description:
Canada’s second-worst wildfire season on record -- The 2025 wildfires burned roughly 8.3 million hectares of forest over five months, with smoke plumes spreading thousands of kilometers and causing prolonged poor air quality for millions across Canada and intermittently across the United States. The summer was characterized by persistent, widespread drought and recurring spells of hot, dry, and windy weather that fueled fires in waves over multiple provinces. 

The most explosive fire growth occurred in May across the western provinces and southeastern Newfoundland. Major evacuations were ordered, month-long states of emergency were declared in Manitoba and Saskatchewan, and the Canadian Armed Forces were deployed for support. Through June and July, smoke became a dominant feature, causing record-breaking poor air quality in Prairie cities and affecting communities from B.C. to Newfoundland with ongoing fire threats and evacuations. While August rainfall brought some relief to the Prairies, the fire danger shifted east to the Atlantic provinces, leading to forest closures and destructive fires in peri-urban areas. As fall approached, the focus returned to western Canada, where late-season heat spurred yet more wildfire activity. The 2025 season underscored a troubling trend of increasingly severe wildfires, marking the fourth year out of the last five with a well-above-average area burned.

Canada’s 2025 wildfire season was the second worst on record after 2023 in terms of scale and impacts.

Casualties
•	Dead: 2 persons in Manitoba
•	Injured: unknown
•	Displaced: 85 000 people (including 45 000 from 73 First Nation communities)

attributes.location_description: Multiple provinces and territories. Extreme drought persisted across British Columbia’s central interior, parts of the Prairies, Ontario, Quebec, the Atlantic provinces, and large regions of Northern Canada.
attributes.description:
Widespread precipitation deficit drives nationwide drought -- Canada experienced intense and widespread drought in summer 2025, with about 84% of the country classified as abnormally dry or in moderate to extreme drought. This included 80% of Canada’s agricultural lands by end of October. Triggered by a low winter snowpack and a hot, dry summer, the extreme drought conditions were a major contributor to the second-largest wildfire season on record. What began as a western Canadian drought in spring evolved into a near-continental scale precipitation deficit by fall. The Western and Northern regions experienced the most severe precipitation deficit throughout 2025, with precipitation amounts 40-85% below normal persisting from spring through fall. 

Agriculture, water supplies, local infrastructure, and river ecosystems all faced severe stress. Reservoirs, lakes, streams, and aquifers dropped to or near record lows in multiple provinces, impacting water supplies for homes and businesses. Some lakes, streams and wells dried up completely. Water supply crises, emergency measures, and ongoing agricultural losses underline the severity of the economic impact for households, businesses, and governments across the affected regions 

Rarity: The geographical extent and the intensity of the 2025 drought in Canada was exceptional.

Major ice storm in Ontario -- From March 28-31, a severe ice storm paralyzed large parts of Ontario and southwestern Quebec. A slow-moving storm system delivered prolonged freezing rain, with some areas enduring it for over 30 hours. This resulted in significant ice accumulation, reaching up to 25 millimeters in some locations.

The considerable weight of the ice snapped tree branches and power lines, causing widespread and long-lasting power outages that affected hundreds of thousands of homes and businesses. At its peak, 380,000 customers in Ontario were without electricity at once. The hardest-hit areas in central and eastern Ontario declared states of emergency, opening warming centers for residents facing dangerously cold temperatures in their homes. The outages also led to secondary problems like flooded basements from failed sump pumps. In the aftermath, impassable roads covered in debris hampered restoration efforts, which took many days to complete, while numerous schools and businesses remained closed.

Casualties
•	Dead: one casualty due to a vehicle collision
•	Injured: Several injuries due to multiple road accidents
•	Displaced: Citizens could take refuge in warming centers due to the prolonged power outages and cold temperatures.

attributes.location_description: Provinces of Alberta, Saskatchewan and Manitoba
attributes.description:
Severe thunderstorms sweep the Prairies -- Destructive clusters of thunderstorms wreaked havoc along a path beginning in southern Alberta and extending across Saskatchewan and Manitoba. Large hail, damaging winds, torrential rain and tornadoes caused extensive damage to homes, farms, crops, and vehicles.
In mid-afternoon, a supercell produced a 400-kilometre swath of large hail—up to tennis-ball size—from Alberta into Saskatchewan, destroying crops and property. A downburst estimated at 165 km/h brought down power lines and transmission towers, overturned vehicles and farm equipment, and killed several farm animals.

In the evening, another cluster of thunderstorms developed farther north in Saskatchewan, producing baseball-size hail and severe wind gusts, including a 155 km/h downburst. Two tornadoes—an EF0 and an EF2—touched down. Homes, trees, and farm buildings were severely damaged, and livestock losses were reported.

Overnight, a slow-moving cluster of thunderstorms brought damaging hail and flash flooding to southern Manitoba, destroying crops. Rainfall totals of 100 to 140 mm were recorded, exceeding a month’s worth of rain in a single night.

Unprecedented late freeze-up of Eastern Canadian arctic sea ice -- [We selected "Heat Wave" as Event Type as nearest representative term in the proposed list of events.] Warmer-than-normal temperatures and strong winds over Baffin Bay and Foxe Basin during the fall inhibited ice growth, delaying freeze-up by an average of 4 weeks and resulting in record-slow sea ice development in the Eastern Arctic. This slow development contributed to a record-low sea ice coverage across the Canadian Arctic in November and December, and by the end of December coverage was 8.4% below the climatological median, representing a deficit of roughly 225,000 km² of sea ice.

Sea ice freeze-up typically begins in early October, forming along the edges of ice that survived the summer melt. Ice continues to develop through October, November, and December until the Canadian Arctic is mostly ice-covered.

Rarity: Unprecedented, latest sea ice freeze-up in modern records (records since 1968).

Prolonged dry conditions affected the Islands Mahe, Praslin, and La Digue during 2025. The three-month Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI-3) at Seychelles International Airport indicated the onset of the dry conditions started during the period of April-June (AMJ) 2025, which persisted until October-December (OND) 2025. The peak of dryness was recorded during August-October (ASO) 2025. January 2025 was very dry in all areas in Israel. It was especially significant in the north where only a few millimeters were recorded (less then 5 mm), making  it the driest January since measurements began more than 80 years ago.

An unusual cold spell with temperature 7-10°C below average. In the northern Golan Heights, minimum temperatures of −6 to −4°C were recorded; in the Upper Galilee and the southern Golan Heights, −2 to −1°C; and in the Judean Mountains, −1 to 0°C.

Major fires in the Judean western slopes during a very warm and dry day. They spread rapidly due to strong winds, leading to the evacuation of many people, road closures, and the cancellation of official ceremonies, including the Independence Day ceremony.

attributes.location_description: Southern Israel - Negev, Arava, Judean desert
attributes.description: Heavy rainfall in southern Israel which was concentrated in several areas in the Negev and the Dead Sea region, and it fell at very high intensities. In the Arugot Stream area, 41 mm fell within one hour, of which 31 mm fell in just ten minutes — exceptional intensities which were very close to the record intensity values in southern Israel.

A heat wave that lasted about a week, during which peak temperatures of 40–43°C were recorded in the Negev and the northern valleys, 44–46°C in the Jordan Valley and the Arava, and in Sedom (southern Dead Sea) an exceptional value of 47.4°C was recorded.

An extreme and prolonged  heat wave with temperatures 7–10°C above average in the mountains and inland areas. At the peak of the heat wave, temperatures of 45–48°C and higher were recorded in the Jordan Valley and the Arava; 46–47°C in the Hula Valley; 43–45°C around the Sea of Galilee; 40–42°C in the Negev and the mountains; and 36–37°C along the coastal plain. The nights were also exceptionally hot. Along the coastal plain, minimum temperatures were 27–29°C; in the Jordan Valley and the Dead Sea region, 33–36°C; and in the northern and central mountains, 30–31°C.

During the heat wave, records were broken: in Gilgal a maximum temperature of 49.7°C was recorded, a station record and the second-highest value ever measured in Israel since the establishment of the state (the highest value, 49.9°C, was recorded in July 2019). In Sedom, the national record for highest minimum temperature was broken with a value of 37.1°C, surpassing the previous record of 36.5°C from September 2015.

Exceptionally large amounts of rain fell in northern Israel. Most of the rainfall occurred in the morning hours, and stations in the Nahariya area recorded more than 130 mm (Regba 144 mm, Evron 135 mm). In the Akko area, about 80–100 mm fell, and in the Zevulun Valley area 40–60 mm were recorded.

The rainfall amounts in the Nahariya area were extremely unusual for September which is usually a dry month.   They exceeded by a wide margin the national September daily rainfall record of 96 mm, which was measured in the Gush Etzion area in September 1932. The rainfall intensities during the event were also exceptional: all the rain in the Nahariya area fell within 3–4 hours, with most of it (over 100 mm) falling in just two hours. Nearly 100 mm fell within an hour and a half, and more than 70 mm fell within one hour. These rainfall intensities are extreme, with an average return period rarer than once in 100 years.

An intense heat wave with temperatures above 30°C  across most of the country, and at times even above 35°C, which is very unusual for November. In some parts of the country, November temperature records were broken (38.3°C in the Coastal Plain, 35.1°C in Qarne Shomron).

An extreme rainfall system which was named “Byron” as part of the severe storm naming in Europe. The system brought very large rainfall amounts, reaching 150–200 mm and more.

Some of the rain fell at high intensities, resulting in strong flows and flash floods in the streams of the Judean Desert and in rivers in the southern coastal plain. In addition, urban flooding occurred, mainly in Yavne and Ashkelon, and rescue forces were required to evacuate trapped people.

The center and especially the north of the country were affected by two intense heat wave events during the second half of the summer. The first episode from January 29th to February 12th. In addition to surpassing some historical records for maximum and minimum highest temperature, this season stood out for the number of times temperatures exceeded 40°C in the country, especially during the last event.
This new extreme heat event had the particularity of developing rapidly within a mass of tropical origin air, reaching its maximum extension on the third day (21/2) and then remaining in the north of the country, gradually decreasing in extension until the 27/2. Strikingly, the event did not break, and from the 28th onwards, it retreated as a warm front, advancing again towards the center of the country, regenerating the heat wave conditions. The arrival of a powerful cold front from March 7 over the central strip, with its advance north the following day, put an end to this extreme heat event, causing several events of heavy or severe rains and storms with significant accumulated precipitation in various areas of the country.

A polar air mass gradually moved from the extreme south of the country northwards, covering the entire continent and producing a marked thermal drop accompanied by snowfalls in several provinces, even where it is unusual, such as Buenos Aires, La Pampa, San Luis, Córdoba, Mendoza, San Juan, La Rioja, Tucumán, and Salta. This situation led to the development of this cold wave event, which stood out for covering a large territorial extension (65% of stations). Some locations established a new annual record for lowest minimum temperature (-13.2°C in Trelew on July 1st; -11.5°C in Puerto Deseado on June 30th; -5.2°C in Concordia on July 1st).

attributes.location_description: North of Corrientes Province and East of Chaco Province.
attributes.description: Throughout this month, the entire northern part of the country experienced several periods of intense rainfall. The north of Corrientes and east of Chaco province were particularly affected, starting on the 20th, where persistent heavy rains caused flooding with severe damages and forced evacuations. Monthly totals between 400 mm and 600 mm broke historical December records in the locations of Corrientes (554.7mm), Ituzaingó (470 mm) and Resistencia (408 mm). Daily precipitation records were also broken (150 mm in Utuzaingó on December 20th and 142 mm in Corrientes on December 25th)

Throughout the year, the Patagonia region, particularly towards the northwest, was affected by mild or moderate drought conditions. An extremely dry winter worsened the situation in this area and 2025 ended as the 2nd driest year on record. The location of Esquel in Chubut province recorded the driest year since at least 1961 with a total annual rainfall of 243.6 mm.

This area of the country was affected by intense events of rain, storms, hail, and strong winds. In the provinces of Córdoba, San Luis, Mendoza, San Juan, and La Rioja, this rainfall was unusual due to the time of year, breaking historical records both daily and monthly. In part of Buenos Aires province, with the soils already saturated, the heavy rains at the end of the month, popularly known as “Tormenta de Santa Rosa” (Santa Rosa Storm), led to new floods.

attributes.location_description: Impacted large parts of southeast Queensland and northeast New South Wales
attributes.description:
Tropical Cyclone Alfred formed in the Coral Sea on 23 February and, after reaching category 4 intensity while well out to sea, turned towards the coast on 5 March, approaching southeast Queensland on the 7th. It made landfall on Moreton Island, just offshore from Brisbane, as a category 1 cyclone on the 7th before moving inland as a tropical low. It was the first tropical cyclone to make landfall so far south in Queensland since 1974. Brisbane received 275.4mm rainfall on 10 March, its wettest day since 1974. There was widespread flash and main river flooding in southeast Queensland and northeast New South Wales, and extensive beach erosion, especially on the Gold Coast. More than 500,000 properties lost power. Total insurance claims to date exceeded A$1.5 billion. 

Report available at https://www.bom.gov.au/cyclone/history/Alfred2025.shtml.

attributes.location_description: Hunter and Mid North Coast regions of New South Wales
attributes.description: Major flooding in the Mid North Coast and Hunter regions of New South Wales after persistent heavy rain. Many stations had daily rainfalls exceeding 200 mm with 337.0 mm at Bellingen (Crystal Creeks) on 22 May. A number of sites had their wettest day on record, including Wingham (278.8 mm on the 20th) and Gloucester (157.4 mm on the 20th), both with more than 130 years of data. The Manning River at Taree reached a record flood level on 22 May, with extensive flooding throughout the region, and numerous other rivers reached major flood levels.

attributes.location_description: Inland southwest Queensland and northeast South Australia
attributes.description: Extreme rainfall occurred in late March in normally arid areas of southwest Queensland, associated with a low pressure trough. Sunbury, between Longreach and Windorah, recorded 602 mm in a week, including 347 mm on 27 March (exceeding its annual average), and numerous other locations had daily falls above 200 mm. Major flooding occurred in many rivers, with record flood heights including those on Cooper Creek at Jundah and the Bulloo River at Thargomindah. There was extensive flooding of numerous outback communities and rural properties, and major transport disruptions. The waters moved slowly downstream over the following months, ultimately leading to the most significant filling of Lake Eyre since the 1970s as Cooper Creek reached the lake in July and August.

attributes.location_description: Northern part of the Northern Territory
attributes.description:
Severe Tropical Cyclone Fina was a remarkable early season tropical cyclone, notable for being the most intense tropical cyclone to occur in the Australian region during November when it peaked as a category 4 system, and for equalling the record for earliest landfall in a season by a cyclone on the Australian mainland when it crossed the Cobourg Peninsula, east of Darwin, on 21 November. It impacted both the western Top End of the Northern Territory and the northern Kimberley in Western Australia. Fina was at category 3 intensity on 22 November as it passed over the southern tip of Melville Island (of the Tiwi Islands) and to the north of Darwin. Wurrumiyanga on Bathurst Is (Tiwi Islands) was the worst affected community sustaining significant damage. The greater Darwin region missed the very destructive core but still experienced damaging winds estimated to 120 km/h that caused widespread power outages mostly as a result of fallen trees.

Further information at https://www.bom.gov.au/cyclone/history/Fina2025.shtml

attributes.location_description: Western parts of southern Western Australia
attributes.description: A significant heatwave affected western parts of southern Western Australia from 19 to 22 January. The hottest day was the 20th, when Geraldton reached 49.3 C, equalling its highest temperature on record. This was the hottest day in Australia in 2025. A number of other locations set January records and Perth Metro (43.6 C) had its hottest day since 2015. Numerous locations also had their hottest January night on record on the 21st or 22nd, including Merredin (30.0 C).

September 2025 was marked by periods of exceptionally heavy precipitation. At two of the three ASTA stations used for the analysis of the bulletin, new records were set, making September 2025 the wettest September since the beginning of observations. In Clemency, the monthly total reached 211.6 mm, the highest total since 1907. With a total of 157.1 mm in Remich, September 2025 also ranks first since the start of records in 1949.

At Luxembourg Airport (Findel), the monthly precipitation total (265.1 mm) likewise broke the record and even represents the highest monthly total ever recorded at Findel since measurements began in 1947. The precipitation amount of 126.7 mm within 24 hours recorded from the 8th to the 9th of September 2025 at Findel-Airport is the highest intensity ever recorded since 1947.

The torrential rainfall occurring over a short period (mostly within 12 hours) at the beginning of September caused flooding in central and southern Luxembourg. The area most affected was primarily the Alzette Valley, with water levels exceeding red alert thresholds on the Alzette at Pfaffenthal and Steinsel and flood return periods of 2 to 5 years. The extreme precipitation was largely confined to the Alzette Valley and did not trigger significant responses in the tributaries; therefore, return periods there were lower. The lower precipitation amounts at the end of September only led to localized flooding

Development and Track of Kajiki Typhoon Kajiki (2025) formed on 22 August 2025 in the Northwest Pacific and propagated westward across the South China Sea. It intensified rapidly and made landfall in central Vietnam on 25 August as a powerful tropical cyclone. Developed from a low-pressure zone east of the Philippines on Aug 22. Strengthened to typhoon status with winds up to ~166 km/h before landfall. Landfall occurred in Nghe An/Ha Tinh region of Vietnam on 25 Aug.  Weakening and Remnant Phase After landfall, Kajiki rapidly weakened over land: The system degraded from typhoon to tropical depression over Laos, yet its remnants continued to carry significant moisture and rainfall. Although wind intensity dropped substantially inland, heavy rain bands persisted and spread wide into Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, and other parts of Southeast Asia due to atmospheric moisture left behind by the storm. Key aspect of the remnant phase: Even after losing cyclone structure, the remains of Kajiki acted like a rain-making machine, driving flooding and landslides far from its original center. 3. Impacts of the Remnant System 🇻🇳 Vietnam After weakening, persistent torrential rain caused severe flooding in central and northern Vietnam. Hanoi and other provinces saw deluges exceeding 150–600 mm in short periods. Floodwaters inundated urban areas, blocked roads, and disrupted power and communication lines. 🇱🇦 Laos The weakened depression moved over mountainous terrain, and its remnant moisture delivered intense rain, raising flash-flood and landslide risks. Authorities suspended rail services and warned riverside communities. 🇹🇭 Thailand The remnant moisture triggered widespread rain and floods in northern and northeastern provinces. Flash flooding and landslides were reported in foothill and lowland areas, especially near waterways. 🇲🇲 Myanmar Local reports indicated that heavy rainfall associated with Kajiki’s remnants caused landslides and flooding in parts of Shan State, including Pinlaung Township. These rains damaged property and caused fatalities. 4. Human and Economic Toll While much of the destruction occurred during the cyclone’s peak, the remnant stage contributed to ongoing hazards: In Vietnam and Thailand combined, over a dozen deaths were attributed to flooding and landslides during/after the cyclone phase, with many more injuries and nearly 100 missing reported. The broader 2025 Kajiki event (including remnant effects) led to significant property and agricultural loss, power outages, and infrastructure damage across the region.  5. Scientific/Climatological Notes Kajiki’s rapid intensification just before landfall was supported by unusually warm sea surface temperatures and favorable atmospheric conditions, which also helped retain moisture even after structural weakening. Such remnants are not just degenerate storms — they often evolve into slow-moving rain systems, especially over complex terrain, exacerbating flood risks. (General meteorological understanding reinforced by observed heavy rainfall patterns during Kajiki’s remnant stage.)

The fire burned over a third of the park, causing direct wildlife fatalities, destroying grazing land, and threatening community livelihoods. The 2025 Etosha fire included significant ecological destruction and an estimated short-term dip in tourism. I was the first of this kind of land fire over the Etosha National Park.

attributes.description: The floods in Windhoek between March 20, 2025, and March 22, 2025, were part of a larger period of excessive rains and flooding that occurred from approximately March 17 to March 25, 2025. During the specified dates, the heavy continuous rainfall caused widespread disruptions, road closures, and damage.

January 2025 was wetter than the 1991–2020 climate normal. At Asselborn (ASTA), January 2025 was the second wettest January since the start of measurements, with a monthly total of 154.2 mm; only January 1995 recorded a higher total. A similar situation was observed in Remich, where January 2025 ranked as the fourth wettest January on record.

The monthly amount of precipitation of 148.1 mm recorded during January 2025 at Findel-Airport (METEOLUX) is the third-wettest value ever recorded since 1947.

The seasonal amount of precipitation of 398.5 mm recorded during Autumn 2025 at Findel-Airport is the wettest value ever recorded since 1947.

At the ASTA stations, no new records were observed, even though autumn 2025 ranked among the ten wettest autumns at most stations.

Two stationary precipitation lines, linked to low level wind convergence and accompanied by thunderstorm activity, affected Tuscany and Emilia-Romagna regions. Cumulative rainfall totals exceeding 150–200 mm were recorded in the provinces of Livorno, Pisa, Prato and Florence. At Florence Peretola, 105 mm of rain fell on March 14th, marking the highest 24 hour total since 1961. Precipitation totals exceeded 100 mm along the central eastern Apennine ridges (191.2 mm at Casaglia, FI). The 24 hour cumulative precipitation reached a maximum of 158.4 mm at Casaglia (FI, 750 m a.s.l.), corresponding to an estimated return period of 50 years.

Heavy rainfall affected northwestern Italy, exceptional for the month of April. In Eastern Aosta Valley average cumulated precipitation  of 300 mm and maximum values up to 600 mm (return period 300 to 1000 years), average of 150 mm (return period 50 years) in the rest of the region. The intensity of precipitation caused the snow-rain limit decrease locally at 500 m in the morning of 17th April, with abundant accumulations on the mountains. In Piedmont the Boccioleto station recorded 417 mm in 24 hours. Total precipitation during the event reached maximum values of 570.6 mm in Trivero‑Camparient and 565.0 mm in Boccioleto. Precipitation amounts, concentrated within just three days, were more than double or even triple the April average in some locations, corresponding to nearly one‑quarter of the annual total.

A depression over the western Mediterranean generated a secondary cyclonic vortex above the Ligurian Gulf, which then moved northeastward while gradually weakening. This pattern caused a marked intensification of east–southeast winds at all altitudes across the region, transporting humid and unstable air masses and producing widespread, abundant precipitation—particularly in mountainous and foothill areas—along with locally intense and persistent thunderstorms. Gares (BL) recorded 163.2 mm in 24 hours, corresponding to a return period of around 20 years. Significant thunderstorms affected the Verona and Vicenza areas, where storms between the Agno Valley and the Recoarese sector persisted for hours, at times producing cloudbursts. The Valdagno station measured 125.6 mm in just 3 hours, a value associated with a return period well above 50 years. Precipitation totals reached 224 mm in Molini (VI), 232 mm at Passo Xomo (VI), and 234 mm in Staro–Valli del Pasubio (VI).

Intense thunderstorms in the late evening of 15 June caused wind damage and, above all, hydrogeological instability in the Dolomites. A storm cell coming from the upper Agordino reached Antelao and triggered a large debris flow along the Rovina di Cancia. This was a debris‑flow event of considerable magnitude (approximately 70,000 m³), among the largest ever recorded in terms of mobilized material. The rain gauge located near the head of the gully measured record rainfall for this area: 52 mm in half an hour, including 40 mm in just 15 minutes.

A long and intense heat wave hit Italy, mainly regions of Piemonte, Liguria, Toscana, Lazio, Lombardia and Emilia-Romagna.  Between June 12 and July 6, Emilia‑Romagna recorded six days with the highest region‑averaged maximum temperatures since 1961.

A powerful cold front advanced towards the center and north of the country, causing not only a marked temperature drop but also several severe thunderstorms and rainfall, highlighting the historic flood in the city of Bahía Blanca, which caused extensive infrastructure damage, thousands of evacuees, and loss of life. In total, Bahía Blanca accumulated 290 mm in 12 hours and 312 mm between the 4th and 8th of March. This exceptional event, with an intensity of 25 mm/h, has a recurrence period of more than 100 years.

attributes.description: A semi-stationary front caused extreme rainfall (between 200 and 400 mm) that severely affected this region. Floods and severe damages were reported in towns such as Zárate, Campana, Vera, and Buenos Aires city and sorroundings, among others. Daily precipitation records were broken in Merlo (125 mm), Junín (117 mm), Gualeguaychú (114 mm) and San Fernando (107 mm).

attributes.location_description: During the period 19–29.VII, whole territory of Bulgaria  was hit by a heat wave (more than 5 consecutive days with a maximum air temperature above 32 °C)
attributes.description: On 22 and 26.VII, maximum temperatures above 40 °C were recorded in 33 and 35 operational meteorological stations, respectively. The hottest period was in the town of Sandanski and the village of Parvomay, Petrich municipality, where a maximum temperature above 40 °C was recorded on 6 and 7 consecutive days, respectively (between 21 and 27.VII), and in separate regions of the Upper Thracian Lowland, 3 consecutive days with temperatures above 40 °C were recorded (Sadovo – 25–27.VII). Peak in temperatures recorded on July 26 and 27 (red code in MeteoAlarm), when temperatures of 41 degrees were recorded in over 10 regions of the country. This summer, heat waves are of shorter durationq than in summer of 2024, but are ubiquitous (with the exception of the Black Sea coast). In July 2025, temperatures above 40 °C are reached more frequently and massively than in 2024.
Fog was reported on the 11th January 2025. Visibility dropped to 100 meters at Bahrain International Airport

This phenomenon had not occurred for April since April 2015, when Dust storm was reported on 1st and 2nd April 2015, reducing the visibility to 50 meters at the Bahrain International Airport, and Dust storm was also reported on the 12th and 13th April 2015, reducing the visibility to 800 meters.
