The strike of Typhoon Wipha from 19 to 21 July 2025 necessitated the issuance of the Hurricane Signal No. 10. Many places were affected by storm to hurricane force winds during the day on 20 July 2025.

The ferocious strike of Super Typhoon Ragasa from 23 to 25 September 2025 necessitated the issuance of the second Hurricane Signal No. 10 in the year. The issuance of two No. 10 Signals in a year tied with the previous record in 1964. Storm to hurricane force winds affected many places in Hong Kong on 24 September 2025.

There were outbreaks of heavy rain in July and August 2025. A record-breaking number of five Black Rainstorm Warnings (hourly rainfall exceeding 70 mm recorded or expected generally over Hong Kong, and is likely to continue) were issued by the Hong Kong Observatory in just 17 days, from 29 July to 14 August 2025. In particular, the Observatory headquarters registered a record-breaking daily rainfall of 368.9 millimetres on 5 August, the highest daily rainfall in August since records began in 1884.

With plenty of sunshine and weaker winds on 15 April 2025, the temperature at the Hong Kong Observatory soared to a maximum of 33.0 degrees that afternoon, marking the earliest occurrence of very hot weather in a year and breaking the previous record of 27 April set in 1956.

Hong Kong experienced the warmest October since records began in 1884 with a record-breaking mean temperature of 27.4 degrees. There were a total of 7 hot nights including 4 consecutive hot nights (daily minimum temperature equal to or lower than 28°C) that started from 16 October, and 4 very hot days (daily maximum temperature equal to or higher than 33°C) in the month, all breaking the respective records for October. In particular, the minimum temperature recorded at the Observatory on 19 October was 28.2 degrees, marking the latest hot night in a year and breaking the previous record of 11 October set in 2017.
With well above normal temperatures in September, October and December, Hong Kong experienced one of the warmest second half year from July to December 2025 with a mean temperature of 26.3 degrees.

With the relative humidity well below normal in the first half of the year and in November, the annual mean relative humidity of 73 percent in 2025 was 5 percent below the normal of 78 percent and on par with 1963 as one of the lowest on record since 1947.

attributes.description: The exceptionally long wet period was immediately followed by a very dry period. Spring and summer 2025 together were the driest for the current reference period (1991-2020) and the second driest for the entire period (measurements from 1833 onwards). During these two seasons, only 184.4 mm of precipitation fell in Ukkel (normal: 399.8 mm). The previous record for the current reference period was 219.3 mm in 2022. The absolute record remains at 133.5 mm in 1921.

attributes.description:
The most destructive and deadly fire ever occurred in Cyprus. It destroyed homes, properties, crops, livelihoods and craft units in the area, large areas of natural environment - vineyards, pastures -. The agricultural sector recorded damages and cultural - churches and ancient monuments -. Burnt land 120–130 km² in the Limassol district, roughly 1.3 % of Cyprus’s land area (according to satellite and official environmental analysis)

Enormous damage. Burnt land 120–130 km². Damages reported in 15 communities. Damages include 706 buildings, homes-many families lost their homes, properties, crops-agricultural sector, livelihoods and craft units located in the area, large areas of natural environment - vineyards, pastures, the cultural sector- ancient monuments and churches were burnt. In this disaster, many animals in pens, productive animals, strays, pets and wildlife were burned. Enormous damage to the electricity network where poles, cables and transformers have been completely destroyed in many fire-stricken communities, where electricity and water supply were interrupted for a few days. Similar damage in telecommunications.

attributes.description: The barometric system “Coral” originating from Siberia affected the whole of Cyprus. It gave snow to the mountainous and semi-mountainous areas, while snowflakes were also observed in lowland areas. Apart from the mountainous and semi-mountainous areas, frost was observed in various areas with low altitude.  The highest mountains on 22-24 of the month were literally frozen since they were in frosty day conditions; both the maximum and minimum temperatures were below zero 24 hours a day (day and night). The lowest temperatures of the Winter of 2025 were recorded on February 22-25, with the minimum temperature of -3.7oC in Athalassa on the 25th of the month being the lowest minimum temperature ever recorded in Athalassa, for the month of February, since the station started to operate in 1983. During the cold air mass days, the maximum temperatures in all areas were lower than normal by up to 5.6 oC to 9.4 oC, while the minimums ranged in all areas by up to 7 oC to 9.5 oC below normal.

A deep low-pressure area affected the North of Italy with strong and moist flows from south, leading to a 24 hours with heavy rain in the most part of Lombardy. Exceptional rain accumulations up to 200-220 mm was recorded north of Milan, leading to widespread floods and damages, especially due to the overflow of the Seveso River. The return periods for 1‑day rainfall over this area exceed 200 years.

The occlusion of a frontal system affected North-eastern Italy, with severe thunderstorms. A total of 200 mm of rain fell in just 10 hours in Lignano. were recorded  in  Lignano.  141.2 mm in 24 hours were recorded at the Trieste Ronchi dei Legionari weather station; it is the highest 24-hour cumulated amount measured since 1967.

attributes.description: Hydrogeological instability phenomena - including flooding, landslides, and mudslides on slopes and within riverbeds - occurred as a result of the intensity and amount of rainfall, combined with concurrent meteorological forcing.
A stationary convective storm, triggered by the convergence of Sirocco winds from the Adriatic Sea, produced approximately 300 mm of rainfall over 9 hours.

Persistent and widespread precipitation affected most of the region. In the west, lower temperatures brought the 0 °C isotherm down to 500 m, while in the east it stayed above 1000 m, limiting snowfall to the highest elevations. From the 23rd to the 25th, totals reached 195.9 mm at Borgo Tossignano and exceeded 150 mm across much of the hilly area between Bologna and Forlì. On the western Apennines, part of the precipitation fell as snow, with depths of 40–50 cm in the highest central peaks and accumulations from 500 m upward. The event caused significant rises in the Idice, Lamone, and Senio rivers, which remained above the highest alert threshold for several hours, though no breaches occurred. The 48‑hour totals ending on the 25th ranked 6th and 5th highest since 1961 for the Lamone and Senio basins, and the daily and 48‑hour totals for the Idice ranked 7th. Locally, strong winds accompanied the event.

Hurricane Force (10 min mean) sustained wind speed of 142 km/h (76.5 knots | 88 mph) and (3-second mean) gust of 184 km/h (99.5 knots | 115 mph) observed at Mace Head, Co Galway around 4 am local time. These observations ares under review, but we expect them to confirmed as the highest national sustained and gust wind speeds on record (since 1942). https://www.met.ie/climate/weather-extreme-records will be updated. Provisionally, 2025 ranks as the second warmest year on record in Ireland, around 0.07°C cooler than the warmest year, 2023.

2025 and 2023 are the only two years in a 126 year record with an annual average temperature above 11°C.

Four of the warmest years on record have all occurred since 2022
2023 ranks first, 2025 second, 2022 third, and 2024 fourth.

Both meteorological spring 2025 and summer 2025 were the warmest on record.
Spring broke a record that had stood since 1945, while summer surpassed the previous record from 1995.
This marks the first time since 1933 that two consecutive seasons have recorded their warmest values in Ireland.

The national temperature record spans 126 years, from 1900 to 2025 inclusive.

It is now 106 years since the coldest year on record in Ireland (1919) and 39 years since the second coldest year (1986).

Wildfires in southern California, several wildfires, including the two largest blazes--Palisades and Eaton fires, consumed more than 200 square km and over 16,000 structures during January. The fires created unhealthy air quality for millions of residents. Dry conditions and near-hurricane-force Santa Ana winds contributed to the rapid spread of the fires during January.

One of the deadliest flash floods in the history of the state o) Texas claimed at least 135 lives. A rapidly moving mesoscale convective complex—fueled by moisture from Tropical Storm Barry's remnants—dropped up to 500 mm of rain in just a few days, causing catastrophic flooding along the Guadalupe River. Approximately 700 square kilometers experienced flooding on the scale of a 1-in-100-year event, with parts of Mason County seeing rarer 1-in-1000-year floods. In Kerr County alone, at least 117 people died, including 27 campers at Camp Mystic. Just days later, additional heavy rain caused rivers to rise again, with 18 river gauges reporting flooding. The San Saba River crested at over 10.3 meters on 5 July, and again at nearly 3.0 meters on 13 July.

Between 15 and 17 May, a major tornado outbreak swept across the central and eastern U.S., with 60 confirmed tornadoes, including several violent ones rated EF-4, with winds estimated at up to 305 km/hr. The most destructive impacts occurred on 16 May, when an EF-3 tornado struck the St. Louis region. Later that night a long-track EF-4 tornado devastated the Somerset–London area of Kentucky. The outbreak also produced widespread large hail and severe thunderstorm winds, caused over 600,000 power outages, and was the deadliest tornado event in Kentucky since 2021.

The month of March was conditioned by the passage of 4 low-pressure centers over the continental territory (Jana, Konrad, Laurence and Martinho) which caused intense and sometimes persistent rainfall patterns for much of the month. Rainfall occurred generally throughout the territory, but with greater intensity in the Central and Southern regions, particularly during the periods of March 6-11 and March 19-21, associated with storms Jana and Martinho. Analysis of the amount of accumulated rainfall since the beginning of March, for meteorological stations with climatological normals, highlights the stations in the southern region of the mainland with values ​​exceeding 300% of the 1991-2020 climatological normal. Mértola recorded the highest percentage of accumulated rainfall (448%), followed by Beja (437%) and Mora (386%). Previous daily extremes (09:00-09:00 UTC) of precipitation were exceeded at nine weather stations on the continent.

The previous highest absolute value for maximum wind gust was surpassed at 10 weather stations between the night of the 19th and the early morning of the 20th. Maximum wind gust values ​​exceeding 100 km/h were also recorded at 15 weather stations. 
Of particular note are the values ​​recorded at the weather stations in Cabo da Roca, 169.2 km/h at 01:30 UTC on the 20th, Fóia (Monchique), 158.8 km/h at 23:10 UTC on the 19th, and Mogadouro, 149.4 km/h at 04:30 UTC on the 20th. The value recorded at Cabo da Roca, considering the total observed data from the classic series and the data from the automatic station, corresponds to the highest value since 1941 for this weather station and is the 2nd highest value in mainland Portugal (highest: 176.4 km/h at the Figueira da Foz station on 13/10/2018).

attributes.description: At the end of May, 14 weather stations entered a heat wave (Table 4), with only 2 ending the heat wave on May 31st, notably Mértola with an 8-day duration. In the remaining weather stations covering some locations in the central interior, Tagus Valley and Alentejo, the heat wave extended until the first day of June. The highest maximum air temperature values ​​were surpassed or equaled at 21 IPMA meteorological stations, with the 29th standing out with 13 stations; some of the surpassed values ​​were recorded in series spanning more than 60 years, such as Alvega, Alvalade, Beja, and Pegões. The highest maximum temperature, 39.9 °C, was recorded on the 29th at the Coruche and Alvega stations.

attributes.description: The heatwave was most intense in the districts of Bragança, Vila Real, Viseu, Guarda, and Castelo Branco, with most municipalities registering more than 10 days of heatwave. During this heatwave, very high temperature deviations from the average were recorded, on some days exceeding 8 °C. On June 29th (the start of the heatwave), 30 IPMA meteorological stations exceeded or equaled the previous highest maximum air temperature values. A new absolute extreme for maximum temperature was recorded in Mora in June for mainland Portugal, 46.6 °C (previous extreme in Alcácer do Sal 44.9 °C on 06/17/2017).

attributes.description: Due to the transport of hot, dry air masses with continental characteristics, a very intense heat wave occurred in mainland Portugal. With the exception of some coastal regions, where the predominant air flows were from the north/northwest, most of the territory, essentially the interior North and Central regions, and part of the Alentejo region, experienced persistently high average surface temperatures. This heat wave, for the period of July/August, was the longest ever recorded for the interior North and Central regions. During this extremely hot period, two IPMA meteorological stations surpassed their previous highest minimum air temperature values ​​(Viseu and Guarda), and one equaled its previous highest value (Lamas de Mouro). The highest maximum temperature, 45.0 °C, was recorded at the Alvalade meteorological station, and the highest minimum temperature, 28.3 °C, was recorded at the Albufeira station, both on August 12th.

attributes.description: As a consequence of high maximum air temperatures and low precipitation during the summer months, there was rapid soil drying, leading to a gradual increase in the area experiencing meteorological drought throughout the territory and an increase in its intensity (Figure 12). By the end of August, almost the entire territory was experiencing drought, and the northwest, interior Central-South regions, and some inland areas of Bahia were in the moderate drought category. By the end of summer, 99.4% of the territory was experiencing meteorological drought. Between September and October, the drought decreased in the North and Central regions, but worsened in the South, and by the end of October, 66% of the territory was experiencing weak to severe meteorological drought.

attributes.description: Between the end of the day on November 4th and the early morning of November 5th, 2025, a highly active cold front passed over practically the entire national territory, associated with a depression whose center was located northwest of the Iberian Peninsula. The cold front consisted of several lines of instability, oriented in a southwest-northeast direction, which generated significant electrical activity and heavy precipitation in a short period of time in various locations. The highest number of lightning strikes occurred between 23:00 UTC on November 4th and 11:00 UTC on November 5th, 2025 (Figure 17), with more than 20,000 lightning strikes recorded in total, including 5,847 cloud-to-ground strikes (1,698 positive and 4,149 negative) and 14,760 intracloud strikes. The sub-period between 05:00 and 07:00 UTC recorded the highest number of lightning strikes.

attributes.description: Storm Claudia - This depression carried warm, humid air with a high amount of precipitable water, causing episodes of heavy and prolonged precipitation, especially in coastal regions, as well as hail and frequent strong thunderstorms, particularly in the southern region, where atmospheric instability was more pronounced. The amount of precipitation in just 5 days exceeded the average for the month of November. Highest values ​​in those 5 days: Vila Nova Cerveira/Aerodrome 323.0 mm, Arouca 280.8 mm, Guarda 229.3 mm.

Storm Claudia caused adverse conditions, with episodes of strong winds, especially in the southern and central interior regions, which were particularly pronounced on the 15th with the occurrence of two tornadoes in the Algarve: Albufeira Tornado - a mesocyclonic tornado due to its association with the presence of a mesocyclone in the parent supercell. It affected the city's campsite and adjacent areas at 10:02 UTC and subsequent times. Analysis of the evidence of the damage allowed for the estimation of an associated instantaneous maximum wind of at least 60 m/s (220 km/h) (>= IF 2, International Fujita Scale, 2023 version, ESSL); Ferragudo Tornado - A tornado associated with the propagation of a supercell, it affected the Ferragudo campsite and a series of locations to the northeast at 11:24 UTC and subsequent times. Analysis of the evidence proving the damage allowed us to estimate an associated instantaneous maximum wind speed of approximately 50 m/s (180 km/h).

July 2025 was the hottest month of the year and the hottest July recorded in Brunei Darussalam since observations began in 1970. The mean temperature reached 29.19 °C, exceeding the July climatological normal by 0.97 °C and surpassing the previous record set in July 2024. The month also experienced 13 Excessive Hot Weather (EHW) events nationwide, defined as periods with daily maximum temperatures of at least 35 °C persisting for three consecutive days, highlighting the severity and persistence of extreme heat conditions.

The forest area in Syria covers 586,739 hectares, mostly located in the coastal mountain range in western Syria. Summer 2025 witnessed a fire season that was the worst in over ten years in terms of the forest area affected by fires, reaching 21,810 hectares, which is about 3.7% of the total forest area*. The weather conditions contributed to the difficulty in controlling the fires, which lasted for several consecutive days at the beginning of July. The extension of the Indian Monsoon Trough coincided with southwesterly currents in the upper atmosphere over the Eastern Mediterranean region, leading to increased westerly wind activity which further spread the fire. The westerly wind activity on July 4th and 5th also caused smoke to spread for hundreds of kilometers, extending to large parts of the internal regions of Syria and reaching the Euphrates River.

Note: The statistics in "Casualities" section are for all fires not only wild fires.

The 2024-2025 rainy season was one of the driest seasons in over twenty years across all Syrian territories, and in some regions, it was the worst in sixty years, with rainfall amounts significantly below their averages, as the rainfall percentage in the current season did not exceed 75% of the climatic mean in most regions. The regions with the lowest rainfall were the Al-Jazeera region and parts of the Southern region, with precipitation less than 50% of the climatic mean.
The drought continued during the autumn of 2025, where both September and October passed without significant rainfall; the precipitation percentage did not exceed 60% of the overall climatic mean for the autumn season, reaching only about 20% at some stations.
Despite the improvement in rainfall during December, where precipitation was around the monthly averages and exceeded them in the southern and eastern parts of Syria, the total rainfall for the year 2025 did not exceed 70% of the annual average in most regions.

attributes.location_description: Dust phenomena originating from both local sources and externally transported sources
attributes.description:
Syria is affected in the spring by the influence of the Red Sea Trough, in addition to the arrival of the Khamsin Depression once or twice annually in the Levant. Towards the end of spring, the Indian Monsoon Trough begins to form and extend into the region, persisting throughout the summer.
In the spring-summer season of 2025, due to the dry rainy season in the region, the frequency of dust phenomena was relatively high, originating from both local sources and externally transported sources. The number of days with dust phenomena in Syria during March and April was 16 days. Their sources varied depending on the causative weather systems, including: North Africa (Khamsin Depressions), Sinai Desert (southwesterly currents), and local or border areas (local thermal depressions). In May, the Indian Monsoon Trough extended over the region, with the dominance of southwesterly currents in the upper atmosphere for most of the summer, which enhanced wind activity. Wind gusts reached over 50 km/h almost daily in the eastern regions of Syria. Coupled with the fragility of the soil due to successive drought seasons and lack of vegetation cover, the Euphrates Basin, the dry areas in eastern Syria, and the Syrian-Iraqi border were the main sources of dust phenomena during May, June, July, and August of this year.

This represents an exceptional precipitation deficit and a clear indicator of markedly dry conditions across the observed areas.
Sarajevo set a new June maximum temperature (Tmax) record on 26 June 2025, reaching 38.8°C, while Tuzla also recorded a new June Tmax record on the same date with 38.0°C.
Sarajevo set a new July maximum temperature (Tmax) record on 25 July 2025, reaching 38.9°C.

attributes.description:
From October 22 to 28, 2025, due to the affects of a cold air mass, a low system weaken from the Tropical Storm Fengshen, the northward shift of the tropical convergence zone, together with disturbances in the easterly winds and the region’s orographic effects, the Central region of Vietnam experienced the combined impacts of multiple hydro-meteorological hazards, especially torrential rains in Hue and Da Nang. 
The locations of the three automatic rain gauges that recorded the highest 24-hour accumulated rainfall from 12 UTC on October 26 to 12 UTC on October 27, along with their corresponding rainfall amounts, are presented in Figs. 1 and 2. Notably, the Bach Ma station recorded an extraordinary daily rainfall total of 1,739.6 mm—the highest one-day precipitation ever observed in Vietnam—followed by the Khe Tre and Huong Son stations, with totals of 1,103.6 mm and 1,070.4 mm, respectively. These stations are located in Hue City, where the annual average rainfall is relatively high within the Vietnam region, ranging approximately from 2,500 to 4,500 mm.

attributes.location_description: The event occurred in the region encompassing the municipalities of the Rio de Janeiro, Teresópolis, and Angra dos Reis.
attributes.description:
In early April 2025, extreme rainfall affected Angra dos Reis (RJ), Teresópolis (RJ), and the city of Rio de Janeiro (RJ) under a high-impact hydrometeorological setup typical of southeastern Brazil’s coastal zone. Cities such as Teresópolis and the Rio de Janeiro recorded daily rainfall accumulations of 235.4 mm and 148.5 mm on 6 and 5 April, respectively. 
An oceanic cold front organized and sustained widespread instability over the state while promoting persistent low-level moisture transport from the Atlantic Ocean. This moisture influx supported prolonged and locally intense rainfall. The impacts were further amplified by the region’s complex topography, particularly the Serra do Mar and Serra dos Órgãos ranges, which enhanced orographic lifting and precipitation efficiency. The combined effects of frontal forcing, continuous moisture supply, low-level convergence, and orographic enhancement resulted in very high rainfall accumulations over a short period, significantly increasing the risk of flash flooding and landslides in vulnerable areas.

Aproximadamente a las 13:30 h del 31 de julio de 2025, se registró un evento  sin precedentes en la zona costera de la región Ica, denominandose como tormenta de polvo. El fenómeno se presentó por el levantamiento de partículas de polvo asociado al incremento de la velocidad del viento a nivel de superficie, en una zona de clima desértico.
El evento se habría generado por la interacción de un sistema de baja presión con un sistema de alta presión (APS) en niveles bajos, favoreciendo un fuerte incremento del viento con dirección perpendicular a la línea costera, característica no habitual respecto a los vientos Paracas, que suelen ser paralelos a la costa. Además, el levantamiento de polvo fue favorecido por un rápido aumento de la temperatura del aire durante la mañana y hacia el mediodía, alcanzando hasta 31 °C en diferentes estaciones de la región, configurando un día extremadamente cálido.
En niveles altos, se identificó una vaguada frente a la costa sur del Perú, que habría favorecido subsidencia. En conjunto, la configuración atmosférica en niveles bajos y altos, junto con el calentamiento diurno, contribuyó al desarrollo de esta tormenta de polvo en Ica.

Entre el 01 y el 16 de marzo, el departamento de Puno registró lluvias frecuentes, con varios acumulados diarios categorizados como “lluviosos”, “muy lluviosos” y “extremadamente lluviosos”. Estaciones meteorológicas como Arapa e Isla Suana reportaron acumulados diarios sin precedentes, con valores de 64.8 mm/día (03/03/2025) y 78.4 mm/día (01/03/2025), respectivamente; mientras que la estación Progreso registró un acumulado de 45.5 mm/día (02/03/2025), considerado el segundo valor más alto. Estos acumulados diarios representaron el 68 %, 62 % y 47 % del total mensual, respectivamente. Asimismo, entre el 1 y el 16 de marzo, el departamento ya había acumulado en promedio entre el 100 % y 200 % del total mensual esperado. 
En la cuenca del río Azángaro, los días 4 y 9 de marzo se produjeron desbordes asociados a caudales elevados, alcanzando valores de hasta 346.94 m³/s, lo que provocó inundaciones en comunidades como El Carmen, San Juan, Sapapujio, Estrella, Cullco Belén y Collana, en los distritos de Potoni y Achaya. Estos eventos ocasionaron daños materiales en viviendas, infraestructura de transporte y educativa, así como el colapso de estructuras de defensa ribereña y afectación a la planta de tratamiento de agua potable, comprometiendo los medios de vida de la población, principalmente en el sector agrícola. Debido a estas condiciones, los caudales se ubicaron en el umbral de alerta amarillo.
En el río Coata, el 12 de marzo se reportó un caudal de 374.91 m³/s, generando inundaciones urbanas en el distrito de Juliaca, provincia de San Román. Las lluvias intensas provocaron el anegamiento de mercados, parques, hospitales, centros de salud, avenidas y jirones, ocasionando la paralización del comercio, afectación al transporte urbano, riesgos sanitarios y dificultades para la evacuación de personas. Este evento fue clasificado con un umbral naranja, próximo a rojo, evidenciando un escenario de alta peligrosidad.
Asimismo, en la cuenca del río Huancané, el 11 de marzo se produjeron desbordes asociados a un caudal de 182.54 m³/s, generando filtraciones de agua, viviendas inhabitables y afectación directa a familias y actividades agrícolas en numerosos sectores y comunidades del distrito de Huancané. La magnitud de los daños llevó a la activación de un umbral rojo, reflejando un impacto severo.

El mes de diciembre, caracterizado por el inicio de las lluvias de verano, tuvo un comportamiento contrario en algunas zonas del país. Desde inicios hasta casi fin de mes de diciembre, se tuvo un veranillo de una duración de 28 días.
 
Desde mediados de octubre de 2025, las lluvias fueron deficitarias en la sierra sur occidental y el altiplano, afectando gravemente cultivos de seguridad alimentaria como la papa, el maíz amiláceo, habas y la quinua, así como los pastos naturales. Esta escasez de humedad en los suelos se concentró principalmente en los departamentos de Ayacucho, Huancavelica, Apurímac, Cusco y Puno, donde ocasionó daños visibles en los campos: marchitez, pérdida de turgencia en las plantas, emergencia irregular de los cultivos y reducción del crecimiento. La situación comenzó a revertirse paulatinamente a fines de diciembre, con la llegada de lluvias que han favorecido la recuperación de cultivos y crianzas, dependientes de los pastos naturales. Cabe señalar que, estas mismas regiones enfrentaron episodios de heladas y granizadas. Estos eventos climáticos extremos incrementaron la vulnerabilidad de los sistemas agropecuarios.

attributes.description: The heatwave and precipitation deficit that affected the entire territory of the Republic of Serbia resulted in severe to extreme drought conditions, as assessed using the SPI and SPEI indices. Jordan experienced a climatologically rare and severe heatwave during 8–14 August 2025, lasting seven consecutive days, with maximum temperatures 6–12°C above long-term averages. The event was accompanied by episodes of atmospheric instability producing localized heavy rainfall, thunderstorms, and dusty conditions. New all-time maximum temperature records were observed at four stations, and new minimum temperature records at eleven stations, including a national peak of 49.6°C at King Hussein International Airport, confirming the extreme nature of this compound event.

According to records of the Jordan Meteorological Department, a low-pressure system affected the area on 28 December 2025. Observations from the Er Rabbah Meteorological Station (Karak Governorate) indicate that more than 20 mm of rainfall were recorded within a 30-minute period, while the total accumulated rainfall reached 37 mm over four hours. This short-duration, high-intensity precipitation resulted in actual hydrological impacts, including rapid surface runoff, localized flooding, and overloading of drainage systems. The event also led to temporary road closures, soil erosion, and flooding in low-lying areas, in addition to slope instability and rockfalls in mountainous terrain due to soil saturation.

Based on verified observations from meteorological stations located in the affected regions, accumulated rainfall during the January–May period reached only 40–55% of the climatological reference normals calculated for the 1991–2020 baseline. The rainfall deficit was most pronounced across the northern and central regions, as well as the southern western highlands. Long-term climatological analyses indicate that these totals represent the lowest January–May accumulations recorded since 1995 at many observation stations within these regions, highlighting a significant spatio-temporal rainfall deficit

Two significant frost events occurred in the Carpathian Basin in the spring of 2025. The first occurred in mid-March, when a wave front zone and then an anticyclone carrying polar air broke into our country with strong winds. Between March 18 and 21, temperatures between -4 and -8 °C developed over a large area at dawn. The second event took place on April 5, when the phenological state of the plants was already more advanced. At that time, another cold front reached our region, the wind strengthened throughout the country, and in some places it intensified to stormy levels. In the early morning hours, temperatures of -2 °C or lower were measured in most of the country.

attributes.location_description: The entire country, especially the Great Plain region.
attributes.description: From mid-April onwards, the lack of rainfall affected an increasingly large area of Hungary. April and May were drier than usual for this time of year, followed by an extremely dry June: during this period, only one-sixth of the 1991–2020 average rainfall fell. By early June, summer crops in a significant part of the country were already suffering from moderate to severe drought. The situation deteriorated rapidly due to the lack of precipitation and the heat; by mid-August, severe drought had developed in the southern and central parts of the country, with some areas experiencing extreme drought. By the end of August, the situation had improved significantly in the Transdanubia region, but the drought continued in the area between the Danube and Tisza rivers and in the Tiszántúl region, only easing by mid-September.

attributes.location_description: The storm caused widespread destruction across the country.
attributes.description: A powerful cold front caused significant damage across several parts of the country. In some areas, the storm winds uprooted trees and broke large branches, while elsewhere hailstones as large as peas or even walnuts damaged property. The extensive thunderstorm system associated with the front brought destructive straight‑line winds that disrupted rail traffic in Budapest and around Lake Balaton, and the capital’s airport had to be temporarily closed due to debris blown onto the runway and water leaking into the terminal. Around Szeged and Debrecen, the roofs of several buildings were damaged, and widespread power outages occurred as a result of fallen trees and downed power lines. The following day, after a brief lull in precipitation, the Mediterranean cyclone named Gabriel generated supercell thunderstorms over the area east of the River Tisza, and its persistent presence significantly hindered recovery efforts.

This was the most significant May heatwave ever recorded in Iceland. The weather situation leading to it was a persistent high-pressure system near the Faroe Islands, which gradually moved over Iceland. This high-pressure system directed warm air from the south and southeast toward the country for several days, and temperatures were far above the seasonal average. Although similar weather conditions have occurred before, this heatwave was exceptional due to how early in the year it happened, how long it lasted, and how widespread it was. Temperatures of 20 °C or higher were recorded somewhere in the country for ten consecutive days, which is by no means common in May. The warmth peaked over the weekend of 17–18 May, when temperatures reached 20 °C or more at about half of all weather stations in the country. The national May maximum temperature record was broken four times in three different regions, with the highest temperature reaching 26.6 °C at Egilsstaðir Airport on 15 May. Furthermore, new May maximum temperature records were set at 94% of all automatic stations that have been operated for at least 20 years.

https://en.vedur.is/about-imo/news/the-most-significant-may-heatwave-ever-recorded-in-iceland

https://en.vedur.is/about-imo/news/analysis-climate-change-made-the-may-heatwave-more-likely-and-more-intense

https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/climate-change-drives-record-breaking-heat-in-iceland-and-greenland-challenging-cold-adapted-ecosystems-and-societies/

The month of May was warmer than ever before. The average temperature in the country was 8.4 °C, well above the previous record from 1935, when the average temperature was 7.6 °C. The month was also the warmest at almost every weather station across the country. In Northeast and East Iceland, the new May temperature records were much higher than the previous ones. For example, the average temperature at Grímsstaðir á Fjöllum was 1.8 °C higher than the previous record there, and at Egilsstaðir the difference was 1.6 °C. The difference was not as pronounced in southern and western Iceland.
The most significant May heatwave on record in Iceland occurred from 13 to 22 May. During the heatwave, May maximum temperature records were repeatedly broken at weather stations across the country. A new national May maximum temperature record of 26.6 °C was set at Egilsstaðir Airport on 15 May.

https://www.vedur.is/um-vi/frettir/tidarfar-i-mai-2025

2025 was the warmest year in Iceland since records began in 1874. The average temperature for the country was 5.2 °C, which is 1.1 °C above the 1991–2020 average and higher than ever previously recorded. Until now, 2014 had been the warmest year nationwide.
The year was characterized by persistently warm weather across Iceland. Temperatures were above average in all months except January, June, October, and November. May was by far the warmest May on record, July was the warmest July on record (tied with July 1933), and December was the third warmest December since records began.
Spring (April and May) was also the warmest ever recorded nationwide. The heatwave that occurred from 13 to 22 May was the most significant May heatwave ever recorded in Iceland.

New national maximum temperature records were set in individual months:
•	New national record in May: 26.6 degrees at Egilsstaðir Airport on May 15.
•	New national record in August: 29.8 degrees at Egilsstaðir Airport on August 16.
•	New national record in December: 19.8 degrees in Seyðisfjörður on December 24.

The year was the warmest at many weather stations, including in Stykkishólmur where temperature has been measured continuously for 180 years, since 1845. There, 2016 had previously been the warmest year. Among stations with the longest observation periods, 2025 was also the warmest year on record in Reykjavík (out of 155 years), Bolungarvík (out of 128 years), Grímsstaðir á Fjöllum (out of 119 years), Dalatangi (out of 71 years), and Stórhöfði (out of 149 years), and the second warmest year in Egilsstaðir (out of 71 years), Teigarhorn (out of 152 years), and Keflavík Airport (out of 74 years).
At these stations, the years 2014, 2016, or 2003 were previously the warmest or similarly warm, varying by region.

https://www.vedur.is/um-vi/frettir/hlyjasta-arid-a-islandi-fra-upphafi-maelinga

The spring (April and May) was the warmest on record nationwide, surpassing the previous record set in the spring of 1974. The weather was exceptionally good, and vegetation began to grow unusually early. It was particularly warm at the beginning of April, and in mid-May, a 10-day heatwave swept across the entire country, the most significant ever recorded in Iceland for the month of May.

July was unusually warm, particularly in Northeast and East Iceland. Nationally, it was the warmest July since records began, tied with July 1933, which was equally warm. Conditions were especially warm on 14 July, when temperatures of 20 °C or higher were recorded at about 70% of all weather stations. The highest temperature recorded was 29.5 °C at Hjarðarland in South Iceland, which ranks among the highest temperatures ever measured in Iceland.

December was unusually warm, with temperatures far above average across the country. Nationally, it was the third warmest December since records began; only December 1933 and December 2002 were warmer. Christmas was particularly warm, when a strong southerly gale passed over the country. A new December high-temperature record was set late on Christmas Eve, when the temperature reached 19.8 °C in Seyðisfjörður.

New October snow record in Reykjavík

Snow began to fall in Reykjavík and the Capital Region on the evening of 27 October and became heavy on 28 October. The snowfall caused major traffic disruptions and severe congestion. On the morning of 28 October, the settled snow depth in Reykjavík measured 27 cm, increasing to 40 cm on 29 October. Never before has such a snow depth been recorded in October in Reykjavík; the previous record was 15 cm, measured on 22 October 1921. 

https://www.vedur.is/um-vi/frettir/mesta-snjodypt-i-reykjavik-i-oktober-fra-upphafi-maelinga

A severe southerly storm passed over the country on 5–6 February 2025. Wind speeds were high and very strong gusts were recorded across almost the entire country, making the storm one of the most severe in recent years. Such weather is not uncommon in Iceland, however, and typically occurs every 2–5 years. Transport was disrupted, and the storm caused considerable damage in many parts of the country. The most severe impacts were observed in the Eastfjords; for example, extensive damage occurred in town of Stöðvarfjörður. The storm was also accompanied by significant lightning activity in western and southern Iceland, which caused widespread damage.

A new national maximum temperature record for August was set on 16 August, when the temperature reached 29.8 °C at Egilsstaðir Airport.

Conditions were exceptionally warm in East Iceland on 16–17 August. On 16 August, the temperature at Egilsstaðir Airport reached 29.8 °C, surpassing the previous August record of 29.4 °C, which had been measured at Hallormsstaður in East Iceland in 2021. This was also the highest temperature ever recorded at an automatic weather station in Iceland and the highest temperature measured in the country since 1946, when 30.0 °C was recorded at Hallormsstaður.

attributes.location_description: Hurricane moved over the western half of Jamaica as a major category five hurricane
attributes.description:
Hurricane Melissa was the thirteenth (13th) named cyclone and the fifth hurricane of the 2025 Atlantic Hurricane Season. Melissa which developed into a Tropical Storm from a low-pressure area in the south-eastern Caribbean Sea at 10:00 am on October 21st , eventually strengthened into a Hurricane at 1:00 pm on October 25th . Melissa then went through a period of rapid intensification and was upgraded to Category 5 hurricane on October 27th at 4:00 am. A total of 58 bulletins and a Flash Flood Watch were issued for Melissa.
Melissa made landfall at 12:00 noon as a category 5 hurricane in the southwestern section of Jamaica near New Hope in Westmoreland with estimated maximum sustained winds of 295 km/h and estimated central pressure of 892 mb.  This is near latitude 18.1 degrees north and longitude 78 degrees west. After landfall Melissa moved north and then northeast over Jamaica exiting over Trelawny see Figures 2 to 8 which shows Infrared satellite imagery of Hurricane Melissa moving across Jamaica. At landfall the hurricane force winds extended 45 km from the centre and tropical storm-force winds 315 km from the centre see Figure 1. 
At 8:00 am on Tuesday October 21, 2025 the Meteorological Service of Jamaica issued a Tropical Storm Watch for Jamaica. Fishers on the cays and banks were advised to evacuate immediately and start returning to the mainland. Other small craft operators within the coastal waters of Jamaica were advised to return to port. Small craft operators who were in port were advised not to venture out. This advice was maintained for all subsequent warning messages. The Tropical Storm watch was upgraded to a warning and then a hurricane watch at 5:00 am on October 23, 2025. The hurricane watch was upgraded to a warning on October 24, 2025 at 8:00 pm when Hurricane Melissa was near latitude 16.2 degrees north and longitude 74.6 degrees west or about 310 km (190 miles) southeast of Kingston, Jamaica. At 11:00 pm on October 28, 2025 the hurricane warning was downgraded to a tropical storm warning on when Melissa was near 19.3 degrees north and longitude 76.6 degrees west, which was about 105 km (65 miles) north of Jamaica. This tropical storm warning was discontinued at 8:00 am on October 29, 2025 and replaced with a flash flood watch as Melissa moved further away from Jamaica. The flash flood watch was discontinued at 5:00 pm on October 29, 2025.
The Meteorological Service's climate monitoring network, comprising of eighty-six (86) manual rain gauges and a hundred and nineteen (119) automatic weather stations, played a critical role in collecting and analysing meteorological variables during the storm. A detailed rainfall analysis from October 25th to 28th indicated that moderate to very heavy showers and thunderstorms affected all parishes and resulted in widespread flash flooding in flood prone areas and landslides in vulnerable areas. The rainfall values for October 25th to 28th were aggregated to give a storm total rainfall over the four (4) days.
Seventy-Six (76) stations were utilized in rainfall analysis, inclusive of the parish mean comparison with the storm total; only thirty-four (34) stations were available for the station mean comparison with the storm total. The wettest station was Knock Patrick (WRA) with a storm total of 817 mm. The highest one day (24 hour) total was 454.7 mm which was recorded at Knock Patrick (WRA) on October 27th . Knock Patrick recorded the parish and station exceedance with the storm total being more than three (3) times the respective means. The parish and station mean exceedance was such that approximately 70% of means were exceeded. The wettest day was October 28th with a mean total 164.4 mm compared to October 27th with mean of 133 mm. The sub-daily rainfall intensities were analysed for 34 stations. The maximum twelve (12), six (6), three (3), and one (1) hour totals were 309 mm at SIRI in Manchester, 214 mm at Mavis Bank, 131 mm also at Mavis Bank, and 60.2 mm at Savanna-La-Mar RADA.  
Forty-eight (48) stations were available for the return period analysis. This analysis compared the maximum 24-hour rainfall during the period October 25 to 28, with the 2, 5, 10, 25, 50 and 100 year return-periods. Approximately 70% of stations exceeded their 2-year return period, 50% exceeded their 5-year return period, 30% exceeded their 10-year return period, a 25% of the stations exceeded their 25-year return period and 17% of the stations exceeded both their 50 and 100 year return periods.
Sixty-Eight (68) stations were used for the wind analysis. Twenty-Seven (27) of these stations measured wind at a height of 2 m, forty-one (41) of these stations measured wind at 10 m. Please note that the 2 m wind is more localized and not necessarily representative of a larger area. The wind damage was extensive and catastrophic especially over the western half of the island. The recorded onset of tropical storm force wind gust was at 7 am on October 26 at Happy Grove High in the eastern part of the island. The recorded onset of hurricane force wind gust was at 7 am on October 28 at Sangster International Airport in St. James. The onset of one hour average tropical storm force winds was at 8 am on October 28 at Mountainside Primary. Twenty-One or 31% of stations exceeded the threshold for one hour tropical storm force average winds. Five or 25% of these twenty-one stations had winds in excess of 48 knots. Sixty-Five or 96% of stations recorded tropical storm force wind gust and twenty-five or 37% of stations recorded hurricane force wind gust. Fourteen stations recorded category-one hurricane force wind gust, seven recorded category-two, three recorded category-three and one recorded category-four. The highest one-hour average wind of 57.4 knots was recorded at Hampden Estate in Trelawny. The highest hurricane force wind gust was 113.9 knots which was recorded at New Hope Primary in Westmoreland. In terms of duration above tropical storm force winds conditions, the maximum duration for one-hour average winds was eight hours at Esher Primary in Hanover, for tropical storm wind gust the maximum duration was fifty-four hours at Mitchell Town in Clarendon, and the maximum duration for hurricane force wind gust was six hours at Savanna-La-Mar in Westmoreland. It should be noted however three stations suffered damage likely before onset of maximum winds, these were New Hope Primary, Lewisville High and Hampden Estate.
Sixty-Five (65) stations were utilized for the sea-level pressure analysis. The lowest recorded pressure was 913.5 mb at New Hope Primary. 
Storm Surge to be updated.

Se tuvo acumulados diarios superiores a 150 mm en MDC, +120 mm en Pespire CH, ~80 mm El Triunfo CH, +60 mm Nacaome, +80 mm en San Juan LP, +60 mm Danlí EP, ~40 mm Gualcinse L, +40 mm Siguatepeque. Al cierre del mes de abril  2025,  se configuró en Cuba un evento de sequía meteorológica de corto periodo, que en sus inicios abarcó el 51% de todo el territorio nacional. El evento comenzó por la región oriental del país y se extendió e intensificó progresivamente hacia la región occidental alcanzando su mayor severidad en los meses de mayo a agosto, con un  91 %, 97% , 99% y 92 %  de área total del país afectada y un total de 148, 164, 164 y 152  municipios, de los 168  que componen al país tuvieron afectación, en más del 25% de sus áreas, por sequía de moderada a extrema, respectivamente.  A partir de los acumulados de lluvia ocurridos en Cuba, por la cercanía y paso por el territorio nacional de la Tormenta Tropical Imelda y el Huracán Melisa, el evento finalizó de forma gradual a partir del mes de septiembre.

El huracán Melissa se formó en el seno de una activa onda tropical que comenzó a moverse por aguas del mar Caribe el día 19 de octubre. El día 21 de octubre, se convirtió en la Tormenta tropical Melissa, y en la tarde del día 25 alcanzó la categoría de huracán. En la noche del 28 de octubre por su proximidad al territorio cubano, los vientos en el oriente del país comenzaron a incrementarse gradualmente, reportándose rachas significativas en varias de las estaciones de la región. Este régimen de vientos con fuerza de tormenta tropical y huracán causaron cuantiosos daños materiales y económicos, tanto a propiedades estatales como de la población. 
En la madrugada del día 29 el huracán Melissa penetró por la costa sur del oriente cubano por el municipio Guamá, en Santiago de Cuba, como huracán categoría 3 en la escala Saffir – Simpson). En su trayectoria hacia el norte, afectó a toda la región oriental particularmente a las provincias de Santiago de Cuba, Holguín y Guantánamo las que fueron severamente impactadas. A su paso dejó acumulados significativos de precipitación que provocaron crecidas en ríos y embalses de la región oriental. En algunas localidades superaron los 400 mm, seguido de un complejo panorama hidrológico en la región oriental debido a las severas inundaciones en el área, lo que trajo como consecuencia que varias localidades demoraran en pasar a la fase  recuperativa.

attributes.description:
Hurricane Erick was the first tropical cyclone to make landfall in Mexico during the 2025 season. Forming on June 16, the system underwent rapid intensification, strengthening from Category 1 to Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale in less than 24 hours.

On June 19, Erick made landfall over Santiago Pinotepa Nacional, Oaxaca, as a Category 3 hurricane with maximum sustained winds of 205 km/h. Historically, this marks the first time since 1950 that a Category 3 hurricane has impacted the Mexican Pacific coast in the month of June.

From June 17 to 19, the cyclone’s extensive cloud bands interacted with a low-pressure trough, generating intense rainfall across Western, Central, and Southwestern Mexico. Notably, the Presidente Benito Juárez station (Oaxaca) recorded a maximum accumulated rainfall of 359.0 mm during this period.

attributes.location_description: The center of the storm made landfall on the northern coast of Veracruz, specifically at the municipality of Tampico Alto (approx. 50 km south-southeast of Tampico, Tamaulipas).  The most significant impacts occurred in the upper basin of the Guayalejo River. River levels rose significantly over a four-day period, affecting the municipalities of Jaumave, Llera, Gómez Farías, Casas, Xicoténcatl, Ocampo, and El Mante in the state of Tamaulipas.
attributes.description:
Tropical Storm Barry formed on the morning of June 29 over the Gulf of Mexico, fueled by warm sea surface temperatures (≥ 29°C), high mid-level moisture content, and low wind shear. The system intensified into a Tropical Storm with maximum sustained winds of 65 km/h approximately 140 km east-southeast of Tuxpan, Veracruz.

Guided by an anticyclonic circulation over the central Gulf, Barry moved northwest toward the coast. It made landfall at 19:00 hours (Central Time) on June 29 near Tampico Alto, Veracruz, with maximum sustained winds of 75 km/h and gusts of 95 km/h.

Shortly after moving inland, the system weakened to a Tropical Depression by 21:00 hours near Tampico, Tamaulipas. By the early morning of June 30, it dissipated into a remnant low over the border region between San Luis Potosí, Tamaulipas, and Veracruz. Despite its rapid weakening, the system's cloud bands triggered extraordinary rainfall in central and northern Veracruz, and torrential rains in central and southern Tamaulipas.

Response Operation: Due to the severity of the event, a large-scale inter-institutional operation was deployed, involving 4,612 combatants from federal agencies (CONAFOR, SEDENA, National Guard), state and municipal protection services, and volunteer brigades. The operation was supported by 4 helicopters (State Government and SEDENA) and drone surveillance.

attributes.location_description: Historia Meteorológica del H. Melissa. - El origen de Melissa se produjo de una onda tropical que el 16 de octubre salió de la costa occidental de África y que comenzó a moverse a través del Atlántico oriental y central, conocida como la principal zona de desarrollo ciclónico del Atlántico, pero las condiciones en esta zona fueron poco favorables para que Melissa ganara suficiente fuerza, dado los vientos alisios acelerados, temperaturas que a lo máximo estuvieron cerca de los 28 grados Celsius, presencia de polvo del Sahara, pero toda esta situación cambio rápidamente cuando Melisa iniciaba su penetración al mar Caribe, donde las temperaturas de la superficie del mar estuvieron entre 28 y 30 grados Celsius, y esto fue lo que provocó que Melissa se convirtiera en un huracán poderoso y de rápida intensificación, comparándose con otros huracanes de intensificación rápida como Otis del 2023 que azotó a Acapulco, Wilma del 2005 y el huracán Mitch de 1998, solo por citar algunos ejemplos de estos huracanes de rápida intensificación que cada vez son más frecuentes, y que confirma lo que se produce por efectos del cambio climático.     Fig.2.- Domingo 19 de octubre, a la izquierda la tropical #48 precursora de Melissa en La Antillas Menores y a la derecha como INVEST 98l. La onda comenzaba a mejorar su estructura de nubes, aguaceros y tormentas.  El lunes 20 la onda tropical #48 clasificada como INVEST 98L, seguía avanzando hacia el Caribe central, y seguía mejorando en su organización, ubicándose sobre Puerto Rico y con su actividad de aguaceros y tormentas al sur de la isla de Borinquen.                                                Fig.3.- Imagen satelital que muestra a Melissa mejorando su organización. Lunes                                             20 de octubre.  El martes 21 la futura Melissa comenzaba a compactar aún más la actividad de nubosidad, lluvias y tormentas eléctricas, mientras a la vez los radares meteorológicos de la República Dominicana comenzaban a captar la actividad de aguaceros y tormentas eléctricas.              Fig.4.- A la izquierda la imagen satelital, mostrando a la onda tropical #48 mucho más compacta, mientras a la           derecha, los radares Doppler de la República Dominicana, ya comenzaba a mostrar lluvias y tormentas en la franja          costera sur, noreste y noreste del territorio dominicano.  Asimismo, el martes 21, finalmente la onda tropical #48 se convierte en la tormenta Melissa y mediante las corridas de los modelos numéricos de predicción relacionados con trayectoria más probable, había alta probabilidad de que el centro de Melissa no tocara el país, y lo más preocupante era el lento movimiento que tendría en los siguientes días, lo que aseguraba la producción de altos acumulados de lluvias en el territorio dominicano, dado su amplio campo nuboso.     Fig.5.- Trayectoria más probable de Melissa de acuerdo a la posición de las 1100 AM del Martes 21 de octubre de 2025. El miércoles 22 de octubre, Melissa continuaba con su lento movimiento, mientras su estructura nubosa seguía organizándose, República Dominicana recibía campos nubosos por los efectos indirectos, y los acumulados de lluvias correspondientes al 21 de octubre, iban respondiendo a los pronosticados, observándose los mayores hacia las zonas sur, suroeste y noreste del país. Este seguimiento mediante los acumulados, son claves para la toma de decisiones en cuanto las probables zonas a alto riesgo de inundaciones, y a los gerentes de recursos hídricos.     Fig.6.- Acumulados de lluvias en 24 horas correspondiente al martes 21 de octubre 2025.  El jueves 23 no se produjeron cambios importantes en cuanto a los efectos de Melissa en la República Dominicana, los principales campos nubosos asociados al fenómeno meteorológico se mantuvieron especialmente en la franja costera sur, suroeste y noreste del país, y, los acumulados de lluvias correspondientes al 22 de octubre, muy similares al del día 21.     Fig.7.- Mapa que representa los acumulados de lluvias al día de 22 de octubre 2025-  El viernes 24 Melissa seguía como tormenta tropical y continuaba con su lento movimiento hacia el oeste, y el sábado 25, Melissa se convierte en un peligroso y potente huracán, a primeras horas de la tarde, cuando se ubicaba a unos 230 km al sureste de Jamaica y a unos 380 al suroeste de Puerto Principe, Haiti, prácticamente, el centro de Melissa ya se encontraba fuera del área de responsabilidad de predicción de la República Dominicana, sin embargo campos nubosos traseros del sistema, continuaban generando lluvia fuertes a intensas como se puede observan en las imágenes de satelitales, Destacar que los modelos numéricos de predicción de alta resolución a 9 km, indicaban con mucha eficacia donde se estarían produciendo los acumulados mas notables.                Fig.8.- A la izquierda la Imagen del canal infrarrojo mostrando a Melissa ya convertido en Huracán, el país lucía totalmente nublado y con lluvias en gran parte del país. A la derecha, el modelo WRF a una resolución de 9 km, mostraba donde se ubicarían los mayores acumulados de lluvias en el país.  Antes de la media noche del sábado 25 de octubre, las temperaturas de la superficie del mar por la que se movía Melissa, ayudaron al proceso de intensificación rápida, y Melissa se convirtió en un huracán intenso o mayor categoría 3 con vientos máximos de 185 kph, Jamaica estaba poco a poco recibiendo el mal tiempo asociado a Melissa, sin embargo, en República Dominicana, la actividad de nubes y lluvias comenzaba a disminuir paulatinamente.   A la fecha, los acumulados de lluvias que provocaba Melissa, era la principal atención de las autoridades de protección civil, que continuaban coordinado los alertas y avisos de inundaciones en el país, y era sumamente importante conocer los acumulados en los últimos 4 días, tanto para tratar de identificar las zona de alto riesgo a inundaciones, así como en el manejo relacionado a las presas de la República Dominicana.    Fig.9.- Acumulados de lluvias en la República Dominicana a 96 horas para                                                   los días 21, 22, 23 y 24 de octubre de 2025.  El domingo 26, Melissa continuó con su intensificación rápida, y al amanecer alcanzó vientos de 220 kph, categoría 4 en la Escala de Intensidad de Huracanes de Saffir-Simpson, Melissa exhibía un ojo bien definido, mientras los radares de la República Dominicana seguían indicando la presencia de lluvias en el territorio dominicano, mientras los alertas y avisos contra posibles inundaciones seguían vigente, pero además en la tarde aumento su velocidad de vientos a 230 kph. Destacar que la República Dominicana solo recibió algunas ráfagas de vientos con intensidad de depresión o tormenta, especialmente en los sectores suroeste del país.     Fig.10.- A la izquierda la Imagen IR, mostrando el ojo de Melissa y ya azotando a Jamaica. A la derecha, el mosaico de radares del Caribe, del cual forman parte los radares de Puerto Plata y de la República Dominicana, indican lluvias hacia el sur, suroeste y aguas adyacentes noreste del Atlántico. El lunes 27 y martes 28, las lluvias en el territorio dominicano experimentaron una notable disminución, sin embargo, cuando Melissa dejaba al territorio jamaiquino, una banda exterior lejana al sureste de centro de Melissa, inicio la descarga de lluvias importantes a lo largo de la zona fronteriza y el suroeste del país, por lo que aunque las alertas y avisos estaban sufriendo modificaciones, los sectores antes mencionados anteriormente, debieron mantenerse lo debidamente informado ante la actividad de nubes y lluvias que se movía conjuntamente con el huracán Melissa, siempre de manera indirecta en el territorio dominicano.    Fig.11.- A la izquierda, imagen que muestra el momento que Melissa golpeaba al este de Cuba, observe la banda nubosa externa lejana penetrando por el suroeste del territorio dominicano, martes, 28/10/2025. A la derecha, sobre Bahamas, Melissa seguía llevando nubosidad y lluvias a parte occidental del país, miércoles 29/10/2025.  Melissa finalmente, se alejo de las Bahamas, y por consiguiente, las condiciones meteorológicas sobre el territorio dominicano se estabilizaron de manera gradual y paulatina.
attributes.description:
Huracán Melissa

Melissa fue un huracán, que, aunque no afecto directamente a la República Dominicana, su movimiento lento, mantuvo a las autoridades de protección civil, que con los boletines meteorológicos emitidos desde el Centro de Pronóstico Nacional del Instituto Dominicano de Meteorología, INDOMET, en un arduo trabajo por unos diez días, y que sus lluvias acumuladas en ese período, fue el principal efecto, y con el cual, hubo que poner su mayor atención. También se emitieron advertencias a las embarcaciones que cotidianamente operan a lo largo del perímetro costero del país, y en sus aguas bajo responsabilidad.

Impacto

Aunque el centro del huracán Melissa no afectó directamente al territorio dominicano, su impacto indirecto fue significativo debido a su lento desplazamiento y a la persistencia de bandas nubosas asociadas al sistema. Se registraron incrementos importantes en los acumulados de lluvias, especialmente en las regiones sur, suroeste, noreste y zonas fronterizas, elevando el riesgo de inundaciones urbanas y rurales.
Estos acumulados superaron en varias localidades las medias climatológicas del mes de octubre, influyendo directamente en la saturación de los suelos, el monitoreo de presas y embalses, y la activación de alertas y avisos meteorológicos e hidrológicos por parte de los organismos de protección civil. Asimismo, se emitieron advertencias marítimas debido al deterioro de las condiciones en las aguas costeras y mar adentro

CONCLUSIÓN

El huracán Melissa representó un evento meteorológico de alto impacto regional, confirmando la creciente frecuencia de ciclones tropicales de rápida intensificación en el Caribe, fenómeno asociado al calentamiento de las aguas superficiales del mar y al cambio climático.
Para la República Dominicana, Melissa constituyó un desafío operativo importante, principalmente por los elevados acumulados de lluvias y la necesidad de mantener una vigilancia meteorológica constante. La correcta interpretación de imágenes satelitales, datos de radar y modelos numéricos permitió anticipar zonas de riesgo y apoyar la toma de decisiones oportunas. Este evento reafirma la importancia de fortalecer los sistemas de monitoreo y pronóstico meteorológico como herramienta clave para la reducción del riesgo climático.

attributes.description: Cyclone Garance passed directly over Réunion on 28 February. The strongest winds, located in the eye wall, mainly affected the northern and eastern regions of the island (gusts of 214 km/h were recorded in Gillot and 234 km/h in Gros-Piton Sainte-Rose). Very heavy rainfall fell on the north, west and highlands, causing flooding. 141 mm of rain was recorded in one hour in La Possession, a record for this station. The maximum swell of 10 m on the north coast, combined with a storm surge of 20 to 40 cm as the centre of the cyclone passed, led to coastal flooding that prevented the rainfall from draining into the sea via the rivers. This resulted in flooding

One month after the devastating cyclone Chido, tropical storm Dikeledi passed 85 km south of Mayotte on 12 January, bringing torrential rain to the south of the island (300.7 mm in 24 hours in Mbouini, a record for this station) and causing flooding and mudflow.

On 17 January, the temperature reached a record 42.1°C in Porto Murtinho. On 4 February, Quaraí reached 43.8°C, the highest ever recorded in Rio Grande do Sul, and became the warmest day of 2025. On the same day, the capital, Porto Alegre, reached 37.9°C, the highest in recent years. On 28 December, temperature in São Paulo was the highest of the year (37.2°C), a new record and the highest in 64 years of observations.
Extreme heat affected parts of northwestern Mexico in August. Mexicali reported 52.7°C on 12 August, the highest temperature on record in Mexico.

The Philippines was severely impacted by several cyclones during the year. The most significant of these was Typhoon Kalmaegi (Tino), which traversed numerous islands in the central Philippines after making its initial landfall on 4 November.

Ragasa was the season’s strongest landfall in the northwest Pacific, reaching a minimum central pressure of 905 hPa and making landfall at near peak intensity on 24 September in the Babuyan Islands in the far north.

The National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC) reported that a total of 10,078,298 individuals were affected by the combined effects of the Southwest Monsoon, CRISING, DANTE, and EMONG. A total of 34 deaths (with 24 under validation), 29 injured (7 under validation), and 7 missing persons (3 under validation) were reported.
